matchSubstring: kpis does the head of payments care
response:
The Head of Payments tracks six core KPIs every week:

1. STP Rate — straight-through processing percentage. Higher is better; sub-95% means manual intervention is eating margin. [REF:pay.kpi.stp_rate]
2. Settlement Latency — average hours between auth and settlement; drives working-capital tied up in float. [REF:pay.kpi.settlement_latency]
3. Chargeback Ratio — chargebacks ÷ sales count; > 1% triggers card-network monitoring programs. [REF:pay.kpi.chargeback_ratio]
4. Auth Failure Rate (AFR) — failed auths ÷ total auths; spikes here usually mean a misconfigured fraud rule. [REF:pay.kpi.afr]
5. Interchange Revenue — sum of interchange amount; the top-line for the issuer side. [REF:pay.kpi.interchange_revenue]
6. Dispute Resolution Time — avg days from open to resolved; long tails point at understaffed ops queues. [REF:pay.kpi.dispute_resolution_time]

These roll up to four decisions: rail choice, fraud thresholds, dispute handling, and acquirer/processor partner mix.

matchSubstring: stp rate down to the source-system field
response:
STP Rate lineage in five hops:

Step 1 — KPI definition: STP Rate = straight_through_count / total_count, computed at the BI layer. [REF:pay.kpi.stp_rate]
Step 2 — Mart fact: fact_payment_auth has columns is_stp (boolean) and auth_id; the BI query SUMs is_stp and divides by COUNT(*).
Step 3 — Source: is_stp is derived in dbt from auth_status_code IN ('00','APPROVED') AND manual_review_flag = false.
Step 4 — Source system: auth_status_code originates in the card-switch (ISO 8583 field DE39), manual_review_flag in the fraud platform (Sift / Forter event).
Step 5 — Field-level: ISO 8583 DE39 → Kafka topic auth.events.v1 → Iceberg landing zone → silver.payment_auth → gold.fact_payment_auth.

Lineage hop count, governance owners, and dbt model names are all kept in sync via the OpenLineage events emitted by the dbt runner.

3NF and Data Vault are both normalised models, but optimise for different things:

3NF (Codd, 1971) — minimises redundancy by enforcing functional-dependency rules. Great for OLTP because every fact lives in exactly one place; every update is one row. Painful for analytics because joins explode and history tracking requires bolt-on Type-2 columns.

Data Vault 2.0 (Dan Linstedt) — separates business keys (Hubs), relationships (Links), and descriptive context (Satellites). Each Satellite is insert-only and time-stamped, so full history comes free. Loads parallelise because every Hub/Link/Sat is independent. The trade-off: 2-3x the table count, queries always need a Point-In-Time table, and BI tools usually hit a downstream star schema rather than the raw vault.

Rule of thumb: 3NF for the operational source-of-truth, Data Vault for the integration layer when you need bi-temporal history and source-system parallelism, and a Kimball star on top for consumption.

Straight-Through Processing (STP) means a transaction flows from initiation to settlement without manual intervention — no exception queue, no human keying, no secondary review. In payments STP rate is the headline ops metric: 99% means 1 of every 100 transactions costs you analyst time. The lift usually comes from cleaner reference data (BIC/IBAN validity), tighter fraud rules so fewer auths fall into review, and reconciling settlement files automatically against the auth ledger. [REF:pay.kpi.stp_rate]

Subrogation is the carrier recovering paid loss from a responsible third party — most often another insurer or an at-fault driver's policy. Two KPIs matter:

• Subrogation Recovery Rate = recovered $ ÷ identified-recoverable $ — should sit at 65-80% in a well-run unit. [REF:claims_sub.kpi.recovery_rate]
• Cycle Time to Demand = days from claim closed to demand letter issued — under 30 days keeps statute-of-limitation risk low. [REF:claims_sub.kpi.cycle_time]

The data lineage runs claim → loss-cause coding → subro flag → recovery ledger; misses are usually under-coded loss causes (the adjuster never flagged the third-party fault).

Actuarial pricing decisions revolve around loss-cost adequacy and competitive position. Core KPIs: Loss Ratio (incurred losses ÷ earned premium), Combined Ratio (loss + expense), Rate Adequacy (filed rate ÷ indicated rate), and Hit Ratio for new business quotes. The pricing data spine: policy-admin (exposure) joined to the claims warehouse (loss) at policy-period grain, with a separate experience-period view for trend. GLMs are still standard for personal lines — GBMs encroach for commercial.

Underwriting decisions split into accept/decline, term/condition, and price. The workhorse KPIs are Quote-to-Bind Conversion, Loss Ratio by underwriter, and Referral Override Rate (underwriters overruling the rules engine). The data pipeline: submission → enrichment (MVR, prior-loss, property data) → rules engine → priced quote. Audit trails of every override land in the governance lake — required by Solvency II and most state DOIs.

Reinsurance treaties shift loss above attachment to the reinsurer. The two big shapes are quota-share (proportional) and excess-of-loss (XoL). Operations care about cession accuracy — the bordereaux must reconcile to the cedant's policy-admin within 3% — and recoverable timing, because cash from a slow reinsurer ties up surplus. The data lineage runs claim → cession rule → bordereau → reinsurer ledger, with monthly settlement files as the reconciliation point.

EHR integration is FHIR-first for new builds and HL7 v2 for the long tail of legacy interfaces. The hard parts: patient identity (MPI matching across systems), terminology mapping (SNOMED ↔ ICD-10 ↔ LOINC), and consent. The minimum viable lineage runs FHIR Patient resource → MPI matched id → enterprise patient master → analytics patient table, with each hop preserving the source-system ids in a crosswalk.

Clinical Decision Support (CDS) layers evidence-based prompts into the EHR workflow. Decisions: alert vs. silent, pre-order vs. post-order, hard-stop vs. advisory. KPIs: Alert Override Rate (above 90% means alert fatigue), Time-to-Action, and Outcome Lift on the targeted condition. The data spine: EHR observation stream → CDS Hooks endpoint → recommendation → audit log. CDS Hooks (HL7) is the modern integration point.

Telehealth ops centre on visit completion and parity. KPIs: Show Rate, Average Session Duration, Tech-Failure Drop Rate, and Post-Visit NPS. Connectivity issues drive the biggest churn — instrumenting WebRTC stats per session and joining them to the visit fact lets you tie quality complaints to specific carriers/regions.

Patient engagement programs are scored on activation and persistence. KPIs: Portal Activation Rate, 30-day Active Rate, Message Response Rate, and downstream Adherence Lift. The data crosses the EHR (clinical context) and the engagement platform (sends, opens, replies); the join key is the MRN-scoped MPI id.

Store ops live and die by labour productivity and on-shelf availability. KPIs: Sales per Labour Hour, On-Shelf Availability, Shrink %, and Conversion Rate. The data fabric joins POS (transactions) to the WFM system (scheduled vs. worked hours) and the perpetual-inventory feed (counts vs. system on-hand). Fresh categories need RFID or vision-based shelf scans because perpetual inventory drifts within hours.

The classic funnel is Visit → Product View → Add-to-Cart → Checkout-Start → Order. Conversion drops at each step quantify friction. KPIs to wire up: Conversion Rate by traffic source, Cart Abandonment, Checkout Drop, AOV, Refund Rate. Identity stitching across guest sessions is the bottleneck — most teams use a deterministic email/phone match plus a probabilistic fingerprint for the gaps.

Loyalty programs trade margin for behaviour change. KPIs: Enrolment Rate, Active-Member Rate (90-day), Incremental Spend Lift, Redemption Rate, and Liability ($ value of unredeemed points). The unit-economics test: incremental margin from member transactions must exceed the points-cost; otherwise the program is subsidising would-be customers.

Trade promotion ROI is the canonical CPG question. KPIs: Promo Lift %, Pass-Through Rate (manufacturer $ that reach shelf), Gross-to-Net %, and incremental volume vs. baseline. The data join: shipments (manufacturer) ↔ POS (retailer) ↔ trade-fund ledger ↔ promo calendar. Most lift estimates come from a causal-impact / synthetic-control comparison against non-promoted stores.

Retail execution is the field side of CPG — making sure your product is on the right shelf, at the right price, with the right POS material. KPIs: Distribution % (stores with the SKU), Share of Shelf, Price Compliance, and OOS Rate. Image-recognition apps replace clipboard audits — the field rep snaps the shelf and the model returns the planogram delta.

Predictive maintenance shifts repairs from time-based to condition-based. KPIs: Mean-Time-Between-Failure (MTBF), Unplanned Downtime hours, Maintenance $ per Unit Output, and PdM Hit Rate (predictions that resulted in an actual avoided failure). The data: equipment telemetry (PLC / OPC-UA) → time-series store → anomaly model → CMMS work order. False-positive cost is operator hours and missed throughput, so the precision/recall trade-off needs the cost of a missed failure to be at least 10x the cost of a wasted inspection.

Supplier quality is graded by Defect PPM (parts-per-million defective at incoming inspection), On-Time Delivery, and Cost-of-Poor-Quality (COPQ) attributable to the supplier. The data spine: incoming inspection results → SPC → supplier scorecard → procurement system. APQP, PPAP, and 8D are the workflow artefacts.

Supply-chain visibility means knowing where every node and shipment is in near-real-time. KPIs: Order-to-Delivery time, Perfect Order Rate, Inventory Days, and Lead-Time Variance. The fabric: ERP (orders) + WMS (inventory) + TMS (shipments) + carrier APIs (tracking) + supplier portals (exceptions). The hard part is multi-tier — your tier-2 supplier's stockout breaks your line three weeks later, and most carriers don't share that.

Value-based care contracts pay for outcomes, not visits. KPIs: PMPM cost vs. benchmark, Quality-Score (HEDIS / Stars), Readmission Rate, and Risk-Adjustment Accuracy. The data: claims (utilisation) ↔ EHR (clinical detail) ↔ care-management workflow ↔ contract attribution. Risk adjustment is a dedicated team — coding completeness drives 5-10% of revenue in MA contracts.

Revenue Cycle Management spans patient access → claim submission → adjudication → patient pay. KPIs: Days in A/R, Clean-Claim Rate (claims accepted on first submission), Denial Rate, and Net Collection Rate. The biggest leak is front-end: bad eligibility checks at registration cause downstream denials weeks later.

5G network slicing partitions a single physical network into multiple isolated logical networks, each tuned for a use-case (low-latency for industrial IoT, high-throughput for fixed wireless). KPIs: Slice Throughput SLA Adherence, Slice Latency p99, Slice Allocation Time. The data flow: orchestrator (ONAP/OSM) ↔ NSSF ↔ AMF/SMF ↔ UPF metrics — joined to the customer SLA contract.

Subscriber billing decisions: rate-plan design, proration accuracy, dispute exposure. KPIs: Bill-Run On-Time %, Adjustment Rate (post-issue corrections), and Days-Sales-Outstanding. The pipeline: usage records (CDR/IPDR) → mediation → rating → billing → invoice → collections. The mediation layer is where most quality issues hide — duplicates, late records, schema drift from a new network element.

Churn KPIs: Voluntary Churn % (post-paid), Involuntary Churn % (pre-paid expirations), Churn Save Rate (retention offers accepted), and lifetime-value-weighted churn. The signal mix that predicts well: bill shock, dropped-call rate near home BTS, support-call volume in the last 30 days, and competitor footprint changes. Save offers are funded from a separate retention budget — keeping the budget under control is its own KPI.

Quality of Experience for streaming is a function of startup time, rebuffer ratio, and average bitrate. KPIs: Video Start Failures, Rebuffer Ratio (rebuffer secs ÷ play secs), Average Bitrate, and Exits-Before-Video-Start. The data path: player SDK → analytics ingest → CDN logs join → edge POP attribution. ABR ladders matter: a too-aggressive top rung causes rebuffer on slow links, a too-conservative one looks soft on big screens.

Ad inventory ops balance fill rate against floor price and viewability. KPIs: Fill Rate, eCPM, Viewability %, and Invalid-Traffic %. The auction stack: SSP receives bids → DSPs respond → winner serves the creative → impression beacon fires. IVT detection (bot traffic) is now table-stakes before any ad-server reporting; otherwise eCPM is misleadingly inflated.

Renewable generation ops track Capacity Factor (actual output ÷ nameplate × hours), Curtailment %, and Forecast Error (MAE between day-ahead schedule and actual). The data spine: SCADA telemetry → time-series store → forecast model (NWP-driven) → bidding system → settlement. Curtailment from grid congestion shows up as a cliff in the output series — usually correlated with neighbouring plants.

Carbon accounting splits Scope 1 (direct), Scope 2 (purchased energy), and Scope 3 (value chain) — Scope 3 is 70%+ of most companies' footprint. KPIs: tonnes CO2e, Carbon Intensity (CO2e per unit output), and Data Coverage % (suppliers reporting actuals vs. spend-based estimates). The data is messy — supplier emissions data quality is the biggest moat for a credible audit.

Grid operations centre on reliability and balancing. KPIs: SAIDI (system-average interruption duration), SAIFI (frequency), CAIDI (customer-average duration), and Frequency Excursion events. The control loop: SCADA → EMS (state estimation) → AGC (regulation) → market dispatch. DER (distributed energy resource) growth makes the historical assumption
obsolete — distribution-level OMS now needs ADMS-grade visibility.

OMS workflows: detect (AMI / customer call / SCADA), confirm, isolate, dispatch, restore, post-mortem. KPIs: Outage Detection Time, Mean Time to Restore (MTTR), Repeat-Outage Rate, and Customer Impact Hours. AMI 'last-gasp' messages cut detection time from minutes to seconds — but only if the OMS can ingest them at the rate of a major storm (10x normal volumes).

Public-safety response KPIs: 911 Pickup Time (NENA standard is 95% < 15s), Dispatch Time, Response Time (call to on-scene), and Closure Rate. The CAD (computer-aided dispatch) is the spine — every call gets a unique CFS id, with timestamps at every state transition. Mutual-aid handoffs are a recurring data quality issue (same incident, two CFS ids, double-counted).

Social-services case management KPIs: Time-to-Eligibility, Case Closure Rate, Re-Entry Rate (clients returning after closure), and SNAP/TANF processing-time SLAs. The data spine: intake form → eligibility engine → benefit issuance → outcomes tracking. Federal performance reporting (e.g. ACF, FNS) drives most of the schema — local agencies layer on their own custom fields.

The classic SaaS KPI set: ARR, Net Revenue Retention (NRR), Gross Revenue Retention, CAC Payback, Magic Number, and Logo Churn. NRR > 120% is the modern bar for top-decile B2B SaaS. The data spine: CRM (deals) ↔ billing (subscription state) ↔ product analytics (usage) — joined at the account_id level. Cohort views matter more than period views; a single quarter can mask the trend.

DevRel KPIs split into reach (docs visits, GitHub stars, Discord members) and conversion (sign-ups from a tutorial, time-to-first-call, sample-app forks). The hardest measurement: attribution of pipeline to a specific dev event. Tracking utm parameters from event collateral all the way through to a closed deal needs a deterministic identity stitch — most teams settle for self-reported source on the sales call.

Chip design (EDA) program KPIs are dominated by tape-out schedule and verification coverage. KPIs: Verification Coverage %, Bug Find Rate per cycle, License Utilisation (EDA tools are expensive — > 80% sustained util is the goal), and Time-to-Tapeout vs. plan. The data spine: simulation farm → coverage DB → bug-tracker (Jira/Bugzilla) → schedule. Coverage closure is a long-tail problem; the last 5% takes as long as the first 80%.

Professional services bench KPIs: Utilisation %, Bench-Days, Days-to-Staff, and Margin per Consultant. Utilisation is target-banded by level (analyst 80-85%, manager 70-75%, partner 50-60%) — going above the band burns out the team and sacrifices investment time. The data: HR (roster) ↔ time-tracking (utilisation) ↔ project mgmt (staffing demand) ↔ opportunity pipeline (forward-looking demand).

Legal case management KPIs: Cycle Time per Matter, Realisation Rate (billed ÷ worked), Write-off %, and Outside-Counsel Cost per Matter. The data spine: matter intake → time-keeping → invoicing → outcome tracking. ALSP (alternative legal service providers) and AI-drafting tools are pushing realisation back up by automating the long-tail document review.

Disruption ops centre on minimising mis-connect impact when a flight cancels. KPIs: D0 (departure on-time), Mis-Connect Rate, Re-Accommodation Time, and Crew-Legality Breach Rate (a crew member rolling out of legal duty hours). The data: ops feed (flight legs) ↔ crew planning ↔ PNR (passengers) ↔ rebooking engine. The replan is a constraint-solve — every minute of delay costs $$ in compensation under EU261/EC261.

Vacation-rental ops KPIs: Listing Conversion %, RevPAR (rev per available night), Cleaning-Turn-Time, and Cancellation-Refund $. The supply side is messy: hosts, channel managers (PMS), OTAs (Airbnb / Vrbo / Booking) — the same listing exists in 3-5 systems with different ids. Channel manager outages are the #1 source of double-booked nights.

Fraud detection trades off precision against recall. KPIs: Approval Rate, Fraud Loss bps, False-Positive Ratio (legit blocked ÷ legit total), and Manual Review Queue Time. The model stack: real-time rules + behavioural model + device fingerprint + velocity check. A lift in approval rate without a corresponding spike in losses is the holy grail; the more common shape is a tightening that costs 50bps of revenue for every 5bps of loss reduction.

KYC/AML decisions: onboard, decline, EDD (enhanced due diligence). KPIs: Time-to-Onboard, KYC Refresh-Backlog, SAR Filing Volume, and False-Positive Ratio at sanctions screening. The data spine: customer record + sanctions list + adverse-media + transaction monitoring. The biggest cost driver is review FTEs — every 1% drop in screening FPR saves a measurable headcount.

Lending decisions: approve / decline / counter-offer at what price. KPIs: Approval Rate, Funded $, 30-DPD Default %, Charge-Off %, and Yield. The credit waterfall: bureau data → fraud check → policy rules → credit model → pricing tier. Adverse-action notices (FCRA / ECOA) require traceable feature-level explanations — the lineage from raw bureau attribute to model output must survive an audit.

Data lineage is the documented path of data from its source through every transformation to its consumption. There are two common levels: column-level (which source column feeds which derived column) and row-level (the operations that produced a specific row). OpenLineage is the de-facto open standard for emitting lineage events from compute engines (Spark, dbt, Airflow). Lineage matters most for impact analysis ("if I change this source, what breaks?") and regulatory traceability ("prove this KPI was computed correctly").

Data Mesh (Zhamak Dehghani, 2019) treats data as a product owned by the domain team that produces it, with a self-serve platform providing the common infrastructure and a federated governance model setting global rules. The four principles: (1) domain ownership, (2) data as a product, (3) self-serve platform, (4) federated computational governance. The trap: most orgs adopt the org-chart change without the platform investment, which devolves into ungoverned silos.

A data product is a discoverable, addressable, trustworthy, self-describing, interoperable, and secure dataset — Zhamak's DATSIS acronym. It has an SLA, an owner, a schema contract, documented semantics, observability, and access controls. The common implementation is a versioned table (or topic) with: a yaml/json contract, automated schema-drift checks, an SLA-monitored freshness check, and a human-readable docs page generated from the contract.

OpenLineage is an open standard for emitting metadata events about data pipelines — start, complete, fail. Each event carries a Job (the runner), a Run (a specific execution), and a list of input/output Datasets with optional column-level lineage. Integrations exist for Airflow, dbt, Spark, Flink, and most modern orchestrators. Receivers like Marquez or DataHub aggregate the events into a navigable lineage graph.

A knowledge graph stores entities and the relationships between them as nodes and labelled edges, typically queried with a graph language (Cypher / openCypher / SPARQL / Gremlin). For data platforms it's the natural shape for catalog + lineage + semantics — a KPI is a node, the columns that feed it are nodes, the dbt models that compute it are nodes, and the edges encode "derived_from" / "references" / "owned_by". Graph queries find impact-analysis paths ("what KPIs depend on column X") that joins in SQL make awkward.

Apache Iceberg and Delta Lake are both open table formats over object storage. They share the core ideas: ACID via metadata snapshots, schema evolution, time-travel, hidden partitioning. Differences in practice: Iceberg's metadata is more engine-neutral (Trino, Spark, Flink, Snowflake all read/write natively); Delta's tightest integration is with Spark/Databricks. Iceberg's row-level update path uses position-deletes + equality-deletes; Delta uses Deletion Vectors. For new green-field builds in 2026, Iceberg's multi-engine story is the deciding factor for most teams.

Straight-through processing is any workflow that completes from start to finish with no human intervention. Outside payments the term shows up in trade settlement, claims fast-track, and loan auto-decisioning. The metric you want is STP rate; the lever is usually data quality at the entry point — every missing reference field becomes an exception.

Agentic commerce is the discovery, authorization, payment, and fulfillment of transactions where AI agents act on behalf of human principals. The 2026 ecosystem stack:

• Identity & delegation — AP2 (Agentic Payments Protocol) defines IntentMandate / CartMandate / PaymentMandate as signed payloads. Visa Trusted Agent Protocol and Mastercard Agent Pay carry an Agent Authorization ID (AAID) in the auth message via the Visa AGI / Mastercard AAI flag.
• Discovery — merchants publish catalogs over Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP) — Stripe, Shopify, PayPal, Square all run public MCP servers.
• Payment rails — Stripe Agentic Commerce SDK and Klarna Agent SDK on the merchant side; Skyfire and Crossmint as agent-native payments infra.
• Authorization — OAuth 2.1 + RFC 9396 Rich Authorization Requests carry scoped delegation grants (max amount, merchant set, expiry).

The headline KPI is Agent-Attributable GMV %. The hardest data problem is intent-grain attribution — a principal expresses intent on Monday, the agent transacts on Friday, and last-click models erase the agent. [REF:ac.kpi.agent_attributable_gmv_pct]

Agent attribution must run at intent grain, not session grain. The minimal model:

1. Persist every IntentMandate as the unit of credit — intent_id is the join key, not the visit or session.
2. When a PaymentMandate captures, walk back through the AP2 mandate chain (PaymentMandate → CartMandate → IntentMandate) and write rows into attribution_link with a model in {first_touch, last_touch, linear, time_decay, ml_uplift} and a 0..1 weight.
3. Default to time_decay with a half-life tuned per category — apparel and electronics tolerate 7-day; groceries should be 24-48h.
4. Surface the multi-touch view in fct_agent_transaction joined to attribution_link; let BI sum weight × amount_usd to roll up Agent-Attributable GMV.
5. Accept that disputes and refunds arrive days later — your attribution rows must be re-keyed by intent_id so corrections fan out cleanly.

[REF:ac.kpi.agent_attributable_gmv_pct]

Five rails are agent-aware as of mid-2026:

• Cards via Visa Trusted Agent Protocol — Visa AGI flag in the auth message, AAID-keyed agent identity, dispute carry-back to the operator under AP2 §7.
• Cards via Mastercard Agent Pay — AAI flag, Authorization Token for Agents, similar carry-back semantics.
• Stripe Agentic Commerce SDK — CheckoutSessions tagged with agent context, SetupIntents bound to a delegation grant.
• Skyfire — agent-native payment tokens with per-call billing; no card-network hop, settles in stables or USD.
• Crossmint — headless checkout for agents; fiat or stables; targets any merchant via a hosted wallet.

Klarna Agent SDK adds BNPL under the same delegation model. PayPal MCP exposes Pay-with-PayPal as a tool call. The underlying ISO 20022 / ISO 8583 messages still apply — agentic doesn't replace ACH, SEPA, wires; it adds the AAID flag and signed mandate hash on top.

AP2 — the Agentic Payments Protocol — is an open spec (Google + Visa + Mastercard + Amex et al., circa 2026-Q1) that defines three signed payloads that flow through every agent purchase:

• IntentMandate — signed by the principal; declares what they want done (budget, deadline, category).
• CartMandate — built by the agent and presented for one-tap confirmation; lines, taxes, shipping, totals.
• PaymentMandate — authorizes the actual debit; references both upstream mandates; its signature is what the network verifies before authorization.

Key AP2 features beyond the mandate chain: AAID (Agent Authorization ID) for cross-network agent identity; delegation chain proof from principal → operator → agent → merchant; carry-back of disputes to the agent operator under §7 when agent fault is established; signal exchange under §6 for cross-network KYA where contractually permitted. AP2 sits on top of OAuth 2.1 + RFC 9396 RAR for the delegation substrate.

Agent fraud looks bot-like by design — high cadence, machine-clean inputs, IP shifts. Don't apply human-trained fraud rules unchanged. The 2026 playbook:

1. Verify identity, not behavior — require AAID + signed operator JWT + AP2 delegation chain. Reject self-asserted bearer agents above trivial amounts.
2. KYA score the agent itself — Skyfire, Visa KYA, Mastercard AAI feed cross-network signals; build local features on top (per-merchant velocity, refund rate, dispute rate by AAID).
3. Step up the human, not the agent — when amount crosses the grant's stepup_required_above_minor or when KYA score is uncertain, fire WebAuthn or W3C Secure Payment Confirmation back to the principal. [REF:ac.kpi.stepup_rate]
4. Bind the payment token — cryptographically tie the tokenized PAN to the AAID + grant_id + merchant tuple so a stolen token can't replay.
5. Cache trust short — KYA score TTL ≤ 60s; subscribe to Skyfire deny-list webhooks and Visa AAID revocation feed; on stale-score uncertainty, step up rather than block.
6. Capture the chain at dispute time — preserve the IntentMandate + CartMandate + tool_call trace so AP2 §7 carry-back arbitration has evidence.

[REF:ac.kpi.agent_chargeback_rate] [REF:ac.kpi.cross_agent_trust_score]

MCP — the Model Context Protocol — is Anthropic's open JSON-RPC 2.0 contract that lets an LLM agent talk to external tools, resources, and prompts in a standardized way. The wire format is plain JSON-RPC 2.0 over HTTPS or stdio.

An MCP server exposes three primitives: tools (callable methods like search_products), resources (addressable data the agent can read), and prompts (templates the operator can compose). For commerce, the canonical MCP servers are run by Stripe, Shopify, Square, and PayPal — they expose product search, cart manipulation, and checkout as named tools the agent can invoke. Auth is OAuth2 plus capability tokens scoped to the specific agent + grant.

From the data side, every method invocation is one row in tool_call: server_name, tool_name, latency_ms, cost_usd, status. The server logs are firehose-volume — millions of rows/day for an active merchant — and the right move is to keep the high-cardinality trace in a hot store and roll call counts into fct_tool_call for KPIs like Mean Tool-Call Cost per Conversion.

Retail Pricing & Promotions covers four interlocking workflows: regular price line management, promo planning + lift measurement, markdown optimization, and competitive price intelligence.

The vendor stack you'll see in a Tier-1 retailer:
• Revionics Lifecycle Pricing and PROS Pricing — elasticity-driven price + markdown optimization.
• Blue Yonder Pricing & Promotion (formerly JDA) — combined price/promo suite still dominant in grocery and apparel.
• Oracle Retail Pricing Cloud Service (RPCS) and SAP for Retail / CAR — ERP-grade price catalog and IDoc-based propagation.
• Eagle Eye AIR and Inmar Digital Promotions (Quotient) — coupon/promo issuance and digital settlement.
• Wiser, Numerator, NielsenIQ Discover — competitive price + promo intelligence (scrape + receipt-derived).

The headline KPIs are Gross Margin %, Markdown %, Promo Lift %, Sell-Through Rate, Price Elasticity, Competitive Price Index (KVI), and Promo ROI. The hard data problem is that 5+ systems each own a piece of the realized price at scan time — the warehouse has to reconstruct that price-resolution decision per receipt. [REF:pp.kpi.gross_margin_pct] [REF:pp.kpi.promo_roi]

KVI — Key Value Item — is the SKU whose price the shopper actually remembers and uses to form their perception of your retailer. Bananas, milk, the 12-pack of Coke, the 2L of bleach, premium dog food brand X. Retailers protect KVI prices against competitors at all costs because mispricing a KVI shifts store choice, not just basket.

KVIs are typically 5-15% of SKUs but drive 50-80% of price-image. They sit alongside:
• KVCs (Key Value Categories) — whole categories where price perception matters (produce, dairy, beverages).
• Traffic builders — high-velocity items with thinner-than-average margin.
• Background — everyday items where shoppers don't track price.
• Premium — items with thicker margin where the brand carries the price perception.

The Competitive Price Index (own_price_kvi / weighted_compset_price_kvi) is the primary scorecard on KVIs and is recomputed weekly against scraped (Wiser, Price2Spy) or receipt-derived (Numerator, NielsenIQ) competitor prices. The hard part isn't computing the index; it's keeping the KVI list current — they go stale within 6-12 months as competitor assortment and shopper behavior shift. [REF:pp.kpi.competitive_price_index]

Markdown optimization decides, per lifecycle SKU, when to take the first markdown and how deep — and then how to chain subsequent markdowns to hit an end-of-season sell-through target without leaving margin on the table.

The core inputs are:
• Weeks-on-hand and weeks-to-deadline (e.g. season end, end-cap reset).
• Realized elasticity at the cluster grain (Revionics, PROS, Blue Yonder all fit panel models).
• Cost — to keep markdown depth from going below COGS unless the inventory liability dominates.
• Competitor moves on the same SKU.
• Optimizer recommendation vs category-manager override (track the Regret Factor on overrides).

What makes this hard: optimizers want deeper-and-earlier markdowns to maximize realized margin in the actual world; category managers and brand teams resist because of price-image concerns. Capture optimizer_recommended vs actual on every markdown row; require a reason_override on overrides ≥10% deeper or earlier; and report Regret Factor on accepted-vs-overridden so the long-term tradeoff is visible. [REF:pp.kpi.markdown_pct] [REF:pp.kpi.regret_factor] [REF:pp.kpi.markdown_recovery_rate]

Promo lift = (promo_units − baseline_units) / baseline_units — but only the naive version. The version that actually drives money is Net Lift = Own Lift − Cannibalized Units + Halo Units.

The three terms each need their own measurement:
• Baseline — what would have sold at regular price absent the promo. Best estimated with a time-series counterfactual (Bayesian Structural Time Series or a Causal Forest on comparable non-promoted SKU-store-weeks).
• Cannibalization — units transferred from a substitute SKU's baseline. Detect via cross-price elasticities at the cluster grain, then debit.
• Halo — incremental sales on complement SKUs (chips during a soda promo, buns during a burger promo). Credit.

A promo with 80% naive lift but 40% cannibalization is mathematically a 40% real lift; the trade dollars are buying half of what the ad team thinks they bought. Tools used: Revionics, Blue Yonder, Symphony RetailAI CINDE, Inmar Promo Analytics, NielsenIQ Discover. [REF:pp.kpi.promo_lift_pct] [REF:pp.kpi.cannibalization_rate] [REF:pp.kpi.promo_roi]

Competitive Price Index (CPI) = own_price / weighted_compset_price, computed at the KVI grain weekly. CPI = 1.00 means parity; CPI < 1.00 means you're cheaper; > 1.00 means you're more expensive.

The weighting is the subtle part. A category manager doesn't weight all competitors equally — Walmart and a regional grocer don't carry the same gravitational pull on your shopper. Weights are usually market-share or matched-shopper overlap derived from Numerator or NielsenIQ Discover panel data.

The data pipeline:
1. Match own SKU → competitor SKU (exact GTIN, equivalent pack, or like-for-like). Persist match_confidence per row; require ≥0.90 for KVI CPI inputs.
2. Pull competitor price observations from Wiser, Price2Spy, Skuuudle, DataWeave, Numerator, NielsenIQ. Filter to in-policy channel (in_store, web, app, marketplace) and freshness window.
3. Compute weighted competitor price; divide own regular (or promo, if comparing on-promo windows) price.
4. Surface the per-week CPI to category managers; alert on CPI shifts ≥3% vs trailing-4-week average.

Good targets sit in a tight band per category: CPI 0.97-1.02 for KVI categories, more room (0.95-1.10) for premium/background. [REF:pp.kpi.competitive_price_index]

EU Directive 2019/2161 (the Omnibus Directive) requires that any 'sale' or price-reduction claim in the EU must display the lowest price the retailer actually charged in the prior 30 days. National transposition completed in 2022 across most member states.

For data engineering this means: every promo creative, web product page, and shelf-edge label must source a prior_30day_low value per SKU × store at the time of display. That's a query, not a column — so you materialize it.

The practical pipeline:
1. Capture every effective-dated price event in price (source_system, price_type, amount, effective_from, effective_to).
2. Materialize a daily view per SKU × store with prior_30day_low = MIN(amount) over the 30-day window.
3. Expose it as a contract column on the price catalog feed consumed by e-com, signage, and ad systems.
4. Audit promo creative against the catalog at publish — fail any promo where displayed-was-price > prior_30day_low.

Fines per misleading promo are non-trivial (up to 4% of EU turnover under the Omnibus regime). Most retailers treat this as a hard pre-publish gate rather than after-the-fact compliance.

Retail Loss Prevention spans five interlocking workflows: POS exception monitoring, organized retail crime (ORC) investigation, return abuse / refund fraud, internal-theft (sweethearting + cash skim), and inventory shrink reconciliation.

The vendor stack you'll see in a Tier-1 retailer:
• NCR Voyix LP Analytics and Oracle Retail XStore LP (XBR) — POS-side exception engines.
• Profitect (Zebra) and Aptos Loss Prevention — prescriptive exception + shrink analytics.
• Sensormatic Solutions (Johnson Controls) — EAS + ShrinkVision RFID; Tyco RFID and Detego Cloud for item-level inventory accuracy.
• Auror — cross-retailer ORC platform with offender / vehicle linking and law-enforcement sharing.
• Appriss Retail (formerly The Retail Equation) — return-fraud scoring at the counter.
• ALTO Alliance — multi-retailer + law-enforcement information sharing standard.
• Zebra Reflexis — task execution + LP audits; Indyme — smart lockable cases + audio deterrent; Honeywell Connected Retail and RetailNext — connected-store inventory and vision foot-traffic.

The headline KPIs are Shrink Rate %, Known Shrink %, POS Exception Rate, Refund Fraud Rate, Sweethearting Incidents/Week, External + Internal Theft Rates, ORC Incident Frequency, Loss Recovery $, and Alert False-Positive Rate. The hard data problem is PII redaction — incident reports routinely contain shopper names, employee SSN-stems, partial plates; tokenize those at the connector boundary and quarantine raw PII in encrypted case-management. [REF:lp.kpi.shrink_rate] [REF:lp.kpi.known_shrink_pct]

Shrink Rate = shrink_dollars / net_sales. National benchmarks (NRF retail security survey, mid-2020s): ~1.6% for general merchandise, ~3-4% for apparel and beauty, ~2% for supermarkets when fresh categories are isolated. The number that actually moves the program is Known Shrink % — the share of shrink with a documented root cause closed in the case-management system. The leading-indicator move is converting unknown to known.

Shrink decomposes into:
• External theft — shoplifting, ORC boost-and-fence (largest single share for general merchandise).
• Internal theft — sweethearting, cash skim, void abuse, no-sale abuse.
• Administrative error — receiving mistakes, RTV reconciliation gaps, markdown miscoding.
• Vendor fraud — short-shipping, fictitious returns, ghost SKUs.
• Damage — destroyed goods reason-coded to known shrink.

The reconciliation pattern: physical count at period close establishes total_shrink. Closed investigations (recovered, prosecuted, written-off) sum to known_shrink. The residual is unknown_shrink. Track the known/unknown split per store × department as the program scorecard. [REF:lp.kpi.shrink_rate] [REF:lp.kpi.known_shrink_pct]

POS exception engines flag suspicious lane-level patterns — sweethearting, void-after-tender, no-sale opens, cash skim, refund-no-receipt, barcode swap, suspicious price overrides, discount abuse. The exception is a signal; the investigation is what builds a case.

The vendor lineup:
• NCR Voyix LP Analytics — biggest deployed footprint at Tier-1 grocers/general merch.
• Oracle Retail XStore LP (XBR) — bundled with XStore POS in Oracle-shop chains.
• Profitect (Zebra) — prescriptive engine that ranks opportunities + exceptions.
• Aptos Loss Prevention — packaged with Aptos ONE for the apparel/specialty crowd.
• Sensormatic, Datalogic lane-awareness, Honeywell HCI, NCR Counterpoint — niche/SMB.

Each vendor outputs scores on a different scale (0..100 vs 0..1 vs low/medium/high). The first integration job is normalizing to 0..1 with per-source calibration curves, then persisting both raw and normalized scores. The Alert False-Positive Rate KPI is your calibration scorecard — recompute quarterly against confirmed-incident outcomes.

Attribution to an employee requires register-session login events (start_session, end_session) joined to register_id + timestamp. Without that join you'll wrongly accuse cashiers who never touched the register — overlapping sessions on shared logins are a top-3 false-positive source. [REF:lp.kpi.pos_exception_rate] [REF:lp.kpi.false_positive_rate]

ORC — Organized Retail Crime — describes coordinated boost-and-fence operations that hit high-CRAVED categories (cosmetics, electronics, fragrance, OTC, baby formula, designer apparel) across many stores in a single sweep and resell through online marketplaces or pawn networks. Three platforms own the ecosystem:

• Auror — cross-retailer offender / vehicle / incident database with deterministic and probabilistic linking across chains. Retailers contribute incidents and consume linked offender feeds; integrations export auror_offender_id back to local incident records.
• ALTO Alliance — industry standard for retailer-to-retailer + retailer-to-law-enforcement case packets. Cases are formatted to ALTO's schema before being shared, and an alto_packet_id is persisted on the suspect so any disclosure can be unshared on request.
• Local CCTV + LPR — vehicle and biometric features are tokenized via locality-sensitive hashing (MinHash, SimHash) so two records can be probabilistically matched without storing raw attributes.

PII compliance is the hard part. Naive replication of suspect names, dates of birth, and license plates across retailer chains violates GDPR Art. 9 and most state LP statutes. Best practice: share only deterministic hashes + categorical incident metadata, require contractual ALTO disclosure consent at the case level, and persist enough metadata to fully revoke a share. [REF:lp.kpi.orc_incident_frequency]

Refund fraud comes in four common forms: no-receipt return of stolen goods, swapped-content return (rocks for electronics), refund-to-fresh-card scheme, and policy abuse / serial returner (wardrobing). The remediation playbook splits across the counter and the back office.

At the counter, Appriss Retail Verify (formerly The Retail Equation) scores every return attempt and recommends approve / verify / deny based on the customer's return history across the retailer (and Appriss' cross-retailer network for opted-in chains). Cashiers see the recommendation only — the score is not exposed.

In the back office:
• Persist fraud_score rows in a separate table with row-level security; expose only the binary recommendation to non-LP marts. Appriss' DPA restricts use to LP purposes.
• Score sources: Appriss Retail (primary), internal XGBoost (incremental signals), Auror offender signal (when present).
• Watch the Return Abuse Rate KPI — flagged returns / total returns — and the Refund Fraud Rate KPI for confirmed fraud / total refunds.
• Customer-level return restrictions live in the loyalty system, not the POS; the deny recommendation routes to a customer-service queue for the next visit. [REF:lp.kpi.refund_fraud_rate] [REF:lp.kpi.return_abuse_rate]

Predictive Maintenance (PdM) is the discipline of predicting equipment failure ahead of time using sensor telemetry and machine learning, then triggering maintenance only when needed — replacing the older time-based or run-to-failure regimes. The 2026 stack:

• Telemetry sources — OPC UA from PLCs (Siemens S7, Rockwell ControlLogix); MQTT/Sparkplug B from wireless retrofit sensors (Augury, SKF); historians (Aveva PI System, GE Proficy Historian) for years of process data.
• Platforms — PTC ThingWorx, Siemens MindSphere, GE Digital APM, IBM Maximo APM, Honeywell Forge, Schneider EcoStruxure, AspenTech Mtell.
• PdM-specialized SaaS — Augury (vibration + ultrasound on motors/pumps), Senseye (Siemens-owned), Uptake, SparkCognition, Aspen Mtell.
• CMMS / EAM — IBM Maximo, SAP PM, Infor EAM consume the advisories and own work-order execution.

The headline KPIs are MTBF, MTTR, Asset Availability %, Predictive Precision & Recall, % Failures Predicted, RUL Prediction Error %, and % Production Loss Avoided. The hard problem is label sparsity — failures are rare, so unsupervised anomaly detection (autoencoder reconstruction error, isolation forest) carries the long tail, validated against supervised failure_event labels where they exist. [REF:pm.kpi.mtbf] [REF:pm.kpi.predictive_recall]

Remaining Useful Life (RUL) is a model-estimated count of operating hours (or cycles, or distance) before an asset reaches an end-of-useful-life threshold. The reference benchmark is the NASA C-MAPSS turbofan dataset; AspenTech Mtell, Augury, and IBM Maximo APM all expose RUL as a first-class prediction type.

The modeling choices:
• Survival analysis (Cox PH, DeepSurv) — best when right-censored data dominates (most assets haven't failed yet).
• LSTM / Transformer on multivariate time-series — best when you have many similar units with run-to-failure histories.
• Health-Index regression (per ISO 13374 'Health Assessment' block) — interpretable, with explicit P-F curve crossing logic.
• Bayesian degradation models — give calibrated rul_confidence_lower/upper which the work-order planner actually uses.

The operational KPI is RUL Prediction Error % — mean(abs(rul_pred - rul_actual) / rul_actual). A well-tuned PdM program lands at 15-25% on critical assets and 25-40% on Class-C assets; below 15% usually means you're overfitting the training fleet's regime. RUL must be reported alongside a confidence interval — point estimates alone get ignored by planners. [REF:pm.kpi.rul_prediction_error]

MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) and MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) sit on opposite sides of the availability equation:

   Asset Availability % = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)

MTBF is the *reliability* lever — push it up by addressing root causes (better lubrication regime, sensor-triggered alignment checks, supplier quality on bearings). PdM directly attacks MTBF by catching failures during the P-F interval so they're intercepted as planned work, not unplanned downtime.

MTTR is the *maintainability* lever — push it down with spare-parts stocking (against the predicted failure window), crew skill, SOP quality, and diagnostic acceleration (e.g. failure-mode classification feeding the right job plan template).

High-reliability programs target MTBF in the 4,000-10,000 hours range for Class-A assets and MTTR under 4 hours. The ratio you actually want to track at the executive level is Planned vs Unplanned Maintenance % — that captures the upstream success of the PdM program in shifting work from MTTR (reactive) to MTBF-extending (predictive/preventive). [REF:pm.kpi.mtbf] [REF:pm.kpi.mttr] [REF:pm.kpi.asset_availability] [REF:pm.kpi.planned_vs_unplanned_pct]

PdM false positives are expensive — a spurious 'critical' alert on a Class-A line can trigger a needless shutdown costing tens of thousands of dollars per hour and erode operator trust permanently. Tighten the threshold and false negatives (missed failures) increase. This is the core precision-recall trade-off, with asymmetric costs.

The 2026 playbook:

1. Calibrate thresholds per asset criticality (RCM A/B/C, SAE JA1011) and per failure-mode severity tier (S1/S2/S3 aligned with IEC 61508). One global threshold is the rookie mistake.
2. Require human reliability-engineer review on first-time fault modes for an asset class before the model is allowed to auto-create work orders in Maximo/SAP PM.
3. Track precision *and* the absolute false-positive rate as first-class KPIs — recall alone hides this.
4. Use cost-weighted F-beta tuning where the cost of FN >> FP for critical assets and FP >> FN for low-criticality ones.
5. Build a champion-challenger A/B at the asset level before fleet-wide promotion; track the delta in alarm count plus operator-acknowledged-false-positive rate.
6. Feed acknowledged false positives back into retraining as hard negatives — this is the highest-leverage source of label signal in unsupervised PdM. [REF:pm.kpi.false_positive_rate] [REF:pm.kpi.predictive_precision]

OT/IT convergence — bridging plant-floor control systems (PLC, SCADA, DCS) with enterprise data platforms (cloud lakehouse, dbt, BI) — is the single biggest delivery risk for a PdM rollout. The Purdue Model says L0 (sensors) and L1 (PLCs) must be isolated from L3-L4 (MES, ERP); IEC 62443 codifies how the bridging is done.

The practical pattern:

1. OPC UA aggregating server in an OT DMZ — collects from Siemens S7 (PROFINET), Rockwell ControlLogix (EtherNet/IP), legacy Modbus drives, plus retrofit MQTT/Sparkplug B wireless. Speaks one north-bound dialect: OPC UA.
2. Unidirectional data diode (Waterfall Security, Owl, Fend) where IT-rated security requires zero return path.
3. Edge gateway (Aveva PI Adapter, GE Edge, Azure IoT Edge) that batches into Kafka/Kinesis with Sparkplug-B-style asset-aware payloads.
4. ISA-95 hierarchical tag dictionary maintained as the canonical asset identifier. Vendor alias tables crosswalk to the canonical asset_id at ingest; un-mapped tags are quarantined.
5. Edge-vs-cloud model split — small autoencoder at the edge for sub-100ms protective trips; richer LSTM/Transformer in the cloud for fleet-wide RUL and fault classification.

Advisories return as out-of-band CMMS work orders (Maximo OSLC, SAP PM IDoc), never as direct setpoints — the safety boundary is preserved.

OPC UA and MQTT/Sparkplug B sit at different layers of the OT-IT stack — most PdM-grade plants run both, not one or the other.

OPC UA (IEC 62541) is the typed, vendor-neutral industrial protocol. Strongly modeled information (Companion Specs like PA-DIM, Robotics, Machinery), PKI mutual-TLS auth, deterministic time-stamping, server-discovery via reverse-connect. Native on most modern PLCs (Siemens SIMATIC S7-1500, Rockwell ControlLogix via add-on, Beckhoff TwinCAT). The default OT-IT bridge protocol in greenfield: an OPC UA aggregating server in the DMZ collects from every PLC vendor and speaks one north-bound dialect.

MQTT v5 (with Sparkplug B on top) is the lightweight pub/sub protocol. Bandwidth-efficient — single TCP socket, retained messages, QoS 0/1/2, Last-Will-and-Testament for connection state. Sparkplug B layers on asset-aware payloads (NBIRTH/DBIRTH/NDATA/DDATA), unified namespace, and store-and-forward semantics. The default for retrofit wireless sensors (Augury, SKF wireless), edge-to-cloud telemetry over cellular, and any case where bandwidth or battery matters.

Practical pattern at a Tier-1 plant:
• Wired control telemetry → OPC UA from PLCs → DMZ aggregator → historian (Aveva PI) → cloud lakehouse.
• Retrofit wireless / battery sensors → Sparkplug B over MQTT → edge broker (HiveMQ, EMQ X) → Kafka → cloud.
• Both feed the same ISA-95 hierarchical tag dictionary, with vendor-alias crosswalks resolving to a canonical asset_id at ingest. [REF:glossary.opc_ua] [REF:glossary.mqtt]

Time-series modeling for PdM is hard for reasons that don't show up in textbook ML benchmarks. The real-world frictions:

1. Label sparsity. A given asset class may see 1-5 failures per year. The negative class outweighs positives 100,000:1. Supervised classifiers need clever sampling (negative subsampling, time-windowed survival framing) or unsupervised back-ends (autoencoder reconstruction error, isolation forest, one-class SVM).
2. Regime shift. Plants change setpoints, raw-material grades, ambient conditions. A model trained in summer mis-predicts in winter. Mitigation: condition-on-regime features (operating mode, ambient temp, throughput) and continuous data drift monitoring with retraining triggers.
3. Sensor drift. Vibration accelerometers age, RTDs decalibrate, oil sensors clog. Model precision degrades slowly over months — the drift itself can look like an incipient failure signal. Mitigation: per-sensor stuck-at / flatline / range-violation DQ rules; weight features by quality_code at inference.
4. Sampling-rate cliff. Vibration FFT at 25 kHz emits 2 GB/hour/sensor; a 5,000-channel plant at 1-second cadence emits ~430M points/day. Storage tiering policy (raw FFT 30 days, minute aggregates 7 years) trades model retraining flexibility for cost.
5. Late and out-of-order data. Historian batches arrive hours-to-days late after WAN outages; features must accept retro-corrections without restating closed work orders. Use bi-temporal joins (valid_time vs transaction_time) at the feature-store boundary.
6. Cross-asset transfer. A bearing model from one pump fleet doesn't carry directly to another. Survival analysis (Cox PH, DeepSurv) with covariates and physics-informed neural networks help; canonical failure_mode taxonomy is required for any cross-fleet aggregation.
7. Cost-asymmetric thresholds. FN cost (a missed Class-A failure) >> FP cost (a needless alert) for critical assets, and the reverse for low-criticality assets. One global threshold is the rookie mistake. [REF:pm.kpi.rul_prediction_error] [REF:pm.kpi.false_positive_rate]

matchSubstring: kpis matter for trade promotion management
response:
A CPG trade-promotion org tracks fourteen KPIs that map straight to its ten recurring decisions:

1. Trade Spend % — sum(trade_spend) ÷ sum(gross_sales). Industry benchmark 12-22%; the headline funding-discipline metric. [REF:tpm.kpi.trade_spend_pct]
2. Promo ROI — (incremental_gross_profit − trade_spend) ÷ trade_spend. Target ≥1.0; below zero the promo destroyed margin. [REF:tpm.kpi.promo_roi]
3. Deduction Rate — open_deductions ÷ invoiced_revenue. Healthy 2-4%; spikes flag broken settlement or unmatched promo claims. [REF:tpm.kpi.deduction_rate]
4. Net Promotion Effectiveness — incremental_units ÷ promotional_units. The cleanest signal for true incrementality. [REF:tpm.kpi.net_promotion_effectiveness]
5. Incremental Lift % — (promo_period_units − baseline_units) ÷ baseline_units. [REF:tpm.kpi.incremental_lift]
6. Forward-Buy Inventory (weeks of supply) — the cap that prevents post-promo baseline drain. [REF:tpm.kpi.forward_buy_inventory_weeks]
7. Display Compliance % — gates pay-for-performance funds. [REF:tpm.kpi.display_compliance_pct]
8. Pay-for-Performance % — committed spend that paid out only on verified compliance. [REF:tpm.kpi.pay_for_performance_pct]
9. Distribution Coverage (%ACV), On-Shelf Availability %, Perfect Store Score, Retailer Score-card (JBP), Trade Stock Build %, and Baseline Volume round out the dashboard. [REF:tpm.kpi.distribution_coverage] [REF:tpm.kpi.on_shelf_availability_pct] [REF:tpm.kpi.perfect_store_score] [REF:tpm.kpi.retailer_scorecard]

The decisions these feed: promo calendar lock, tactic mix (off-invoice vs scan-down vs bill-back vs MCB), discount depth, fund allocation across accounts, deduction dispute, forward-buy cap, display credit, kill/pull-forward, new-item slotting, and cannibalization guardrails.

Promo ROI in CPG trade promotion management is defined as:

  Promo ROI = (incremental_gross_profit − trade_spend) ÷ trade_spend

Three numbers, three pitfalls.

• incremental_gross_profit — depends on the baseline model you trust. Circana Unify, NielsenIQ Connect, T-Pro Predictive, and an in-house GLM/ML will disagree by 5-30% on the no-promo counterfactual; a fair ROI persists all baselines side-by-side with model_name + model_version and locks one canonical policy per account × category with sign-off. [REF:tpm.kpi.baseline_volume]
• trade_spend — must include off-invoice + scan-down + bill-back + MCB + slotting + display funds + pay-for-performance accruals. The reconciliation gap between planned (SAP TPM) and actual (SAP S/4 SD/FI deductions, credit memos, MCB cheques) is where ROI calculations quietly break.
• Both numbers should be net of cannibalization and halo, evaluated at the brand-basket grain. A 2-for-$5 promo on a 12-pack that cannibalizes the 6-pack should not get credit for the 12-pack lift in isolation.

Typical CPG target is Promo ROI ≥ 1.0; a 'hero' promotion runs 2.0+; a structural under-performer (forward-buy heavy with no pull-through) lands below zero. [REF:tpm.kpi.promo_roi]

Deduction leakage — retailer chargebacks that quietly inflate trade rate because they were paid without being matched to a promotion — is the single largest controllable margin leak in CPG.

Detection pattern:

1. Match every deduction line item to a tactic_id. Walmart issues lump-sum deductions of $200-500k that cover 50-80 SKUs across 4-12 weeks; auto-matching uses a probabilistic matcher (BluePlanner Live Trade Management, Vistex Match Engine) with confidence scores. [REF:src.blueplanner] [REF:src.vistex_tpm]
2. Track the unmatched residual. Any deduction with no tactic_id after 30 days lands in the 'unmapped trade' bucket — this is the leakage pool. Aim < 5% of total deductions.
3. Aging rule. Open deductions > 60 days aging on validated tactics are dispute-eligible; > 120 days they fall off the dispute window and become permanent leakage.
4. Validation evidence chain. For pay-for-performance deductions, the evidence is a display photo / scan-flag / audit log in a unified compliance event log keyed by (outlet_id, tactic_id, week_start). Missing evidence ⇒ withhold. [REF:tpm.kpi.pay_for_performance_pct]
5. Score-card surfacing. Deduction Rate (open_deductions ÷ invoiced_revenue) at account × promo grain. Spikes above the 2-4% healthy band trigger an analyst-review queue. [REF:tpm.kpi.deduction_rate]

The operational unit owning this: Deductions Manager + Trade Spend Analyst, escalating to Finance Controller (Trade) for write-off authorization.

Off-invoice and scan-down are two of the four canonical trade-promo settlement methods (alongside bill-back and MCB).

Off-Invoice — the discount is applied as a per-case allowance directly on the manufacturer's invoice at ship time. Cash is the simplest treatment; no reconciliation is needed because the discount is netted on the invoice itself. Downsides: zero performance gate (you fund the discount whether the retailer displays/features or not), and it creates forward-buy exposure — retailers buy 10 weeks of supply during the promo window and drain shelves at full price afterwards. [REF:tpm.kpi.trade_stock_build_pct]

Scan-Down — the manufacturer credits the retailer for each promoted unit actually scanned at point-of-sale during the promo window. Reconciliation comes from EDI 852 / Circana / NielsenIQ scan feeds. Upside: ties spend to actual consumer movement, almost no forward-buy exposure. Downside: 30-90 day cash delay, scan-data scope mismatch (EDI 852 covers a subset of stores; Circana/NielsenIQ project at higher latency), and elevated dispute rate when retailer scan totals disagree with manufacturer auditor scan totals. [REF:tpm.kpi.deduction_rate]

The decision: prefer scan-down for hero SKUs and accounts where consumer pull-through is the question; use off-invoice for everyday-low-price commitments and accounts with clean compliance history; use bill-back when pay-for-performance gating is required; use MCB when a wholesaler is involved between you and the retailer. [REF:tpm.d.tactic_mix]

Forward buy (also called trade loading or pre-buy) is the retailer's deliberate ordering of additional inventory during a promo window to lock in the discount and drain shelves at full price afterwards. It is the classic source of CPG bullwhip — inflated short-term shipments followed by depressed post-promo baseline.

Two KPIs guard against it:

• Trade Stock Build % — (forward_buy_units − normal_purchase_units) ÷ normal_purchase_units. Captures the order-volume uplift attributable to forward-buy, not real consumer movement. [REF:tpm.kpi.trade_stock_build_pct]
• Forward-Buy Inventory (weeks of supply) — forward_buy_units ÷ weekly_baseline_units. Set a hard cap (typically 4-6 weeks) per account per promo so the promotion does not drain too many weeks of baseline. [REF:tpm.kpi.forward_buy_inventory_weeks]

The downstream distortion that forward-buy creates in baseline forecasting is itself a data-quality problem: the next-period baseline model interprets the post-promo draw-down as decay and silently lowers future plan targets. The fix: tag inventory weeks downstream of a known promo as 'forward-buy adjusted', re-train baselines on a pre-promo window only, and surface trade_stock_build_pct as a guardrail on every plan. The decision that uses these signals is the Forward Buy Cap [REF:tpm.d.forward_buy_cap].

EDI 852 (ASC X12 Product Activity Data) is the weekly POS movement and on-hand inventory feed retailers send manufacturers. It's the early-signal feed for in-flight promo monitoring — typically arrives 2-5 days after week-close, vs the 8-week latency on Circana / NielsenIQ syndicated panels.

What it carries: SKU (GTIN-14), store/banner, week_start_date, units_sold, dollars_sold, on_hand_units, plus optional on_promo / feature / display / TPR flags. Cadence varies wildly — Walmart sends daily, Kroger weekly, regional grocers monthly, with frequent late files. The parser must reconcile partial weeks back-fill and tag every row with source_doc so downstream lift_observation knows whether to use 852 for in-flight or panel data for closeout. [REF:tpm.kpi.on_shelf_availability_pct]

The companion: EDI 867 (Product Transfer & Resale Report) reports sell-through to consumer — used to verify scan-down claims and reconcile sell-through. EDI 820 (Payment Order/Remittance) carries the deduction settlement lines that close the loop on funded spend. [REF:tpm.kpi.deduction_rate]

Integration architecture: AS2 (PKI auth) from the retailer's VAN to a SFTP landing zone, parsed by an X12 reader (typically Sterling B2B, IBM Sterling, or a Spark-on-Iceberg pipeline), normalized into trade_promotion_management.retailer_scan_data with source_doc tagged so downstream models can blend properly.

A modern retail Customer Loyalty + CDP stack stitches four jobs into one analytical fabric: identity resolution, segmentation, lifecycle orchestration, and loyalty program management.

The vendor stack you'll see in a Tier-1 retailer:
• Identity-resolution CDPs — Salesforce Data Cloud (Genie), Adobe RT-CDP, Treasure Data, mParticle, Tealium AudienceStream, Twilio Segment, Amperity. Amperity is the gold standard for probabilistic stitching at retail scale; Salesforce/Adobe are dominant when the org already runs that suite.
• Activation / orchestration — Klaviyo (owned-channel CRM), Braze (cross-channel + Liquid), Iterable, Cordial, BlueShift, Twilio Engage.
• Warehouse-native variants — ActionIQ + reverse-ETL (Hightouch / Census) for orgs that want the warehouse to remain the source of truth.
• Loyalty engines — Eagle Eye AIR (POS + online coupons), Annex Cloud, Comarch (telco + retail), LoyaltyLion + Smile.io for Shopify.

Headline KPIs: CLV, NPS, Repeat Purchase Rate, Active Member %, Reward Redemption Rate, Identity Match Rate (deterministic + probabilistic), Churn Rate, Member-Driven Revenue %. The hardest data problem is identity resolution — deterministic-only is precise but recalls only ~30% of anonymous web sessions; probabilistic raises recall but pollutes golden records below a tuned confidence threshold. [REF:cdp.kpi.clv] [REF:cdp.kpi.identity_match_rate_det]

Identity resolution is the operation that takes a flood of observed identifiers — hashed emails, hashed phones, loyalty IDs, device IDs, ad IDs (MAID), cookies, postal addresses, wallet passes — and resolves them to a single canonical customer_master record.

Two techniques, mostly run together:

1. Deterministic — merge on a shared, high-confidence identifier (authenticated login event, loyalty_id scan at POS, hashed email match across two source systems). Precision near 1.0. Recall capped by the share of sessions that actually authenticate — typically <30% of anonymous web traffic ever signs in.

2. Probabilistic — merge on inferred signals (IP, user agent, device fingerprint, behavioral pattern, postal proximity) above a tuned confidence threshold. Recall climbs into the 60-90% range, but every merge below threshold polluthes the golden record downstream.

The production pattern: hybrid. Deterministic edges always merge. Probabilistic edges merge only above ~0.85 confidence. Persist match_method and match_confidence per identity_link row so downstream BI can filter (e.g. CLV computed only from confidence ≥ 0.90 customers).

Vendor split: Amperity ships ML-stitch as their core product, Adobe RT-CDP and Salesforce Data Cloud bundle it, Tealium and Segment lean deterministic. The hardest org problem isn't picking a vendor — it's tuning the merge confidence threshold and surfacing low-confidence merges for human review every week. [REF:cdp.kpi.identity_match_rate_det] [REF:cdp.kpi.identity_match_rate_prob]

Loyalty tier design is a margin-versus-aspiration tradeoff. Set thresholds too aggressive and the bulk of members stay at the bottom tier and disengage; set them too soft and the program degenerates into a universal discount without aspirational pull.

The core questions you answer at design time:
• What's the threshold metric? Spend (Sephora's Beauty Insider model) or earned points (most airlines, most generic retail). Spend is simpler but invites discount-arbitrage; points let you weight categories.
• Anniversary vs rolling 12-month? Anniversary is operationally cheaper; rolling drives smoother engagement and reduces seasonal pull-forward.
• Tier benefits? Earn multipliers (1.25×, 1.5×, 2× per tier), free shipping, early access, exclusive products, partner perks, status-only benefits (concierge, lounge).
• Tier-distribution target? Most retailers target ~70/20/8/2 (bronze/silver/gold/platinum) — top tier should feel selective.

The data side: simulate the proposed tier distribution against the last 24 months of customer-spend data before changing thresholds. A/B test mechanic changes on a held-out cohort. Track tier-progression rate (% advancing per period) and tier-downgrade rate as headline KPIs. Persist tier_anchor_date so anniversary-based windows are auditable. Reconcile the points_ledger to the GL nightly — points liability under ASC 606 sits on the balance sheet. [REF:cdp.kpi.loyalty_member_pct] [REF:cdp.kpi.member_driven_revenue_pct]

Consent management for a CDP is harder than the marketing layer makes it sound, because every record needs a defensible lawful basis and a way to propagate suppressions within hours.

The scaffolding:

1. Persist consent_record as a legal book of record — append-only, IAB TCF v2.2 or GPP-encoded consent_string, purpose_codes JSON, jurisdiction (EU_GDPR / UK_GDPR / US_CCPA / US_CPRA / CA_PIPEDA / BR_LGPD), and consent_basis (consent / contract / legitimate_interest / legal_obligation per GDPR Art. 6).

2. Make consent state a first-class join in every audience query. A segment build that ignores opt-out state is one regulator complaint away from a fine.

3. On right-to-delete (GDPR Art. 17, CCPA §1798.105) — flag the customer_master status as 'right_to_be_forgotten', stop new event ingestion against the customer_id, and broadcast a suppression webhook to every downstream destination (Meta CAPI, Google audiences, Klaviyo, Braze, Iterable). The 30-day clock is hard, not soft.

4. Operate brand-scoped CDPs within a holding company portfolio. Cross-brand sharing needs explicit, purpose-coded consent; brand-level opt-outs must be honored separately from group-level. Document the data-flow map for Art. 30 records of processing.

Vendor capability gap: most CDPs have consent storage; very few enforce consent state on every audience query at runtime. Build the enforcement layer yourself if needed.

RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary) is the workhorse segmentation method for retail because it explains a large share of CLV variance with three columns and a few SQL window functions.

The canonical recipe:
• Recency — days since last purchase, bucketed into quintiles (1=worst, 5=best). NTILE(5) OVER (ORDER BY last_purchase_ts DESC).
• Frequency — number of orders in the trailing 12 months, quintile-bucketed.
• Monetary — net spend in the trailing 12 months, quintile-bucketed.

The 125 RFM cells collapse into 9-11 nameable segments — Champions (5/5/5), Loyal Customers (4-5/4-5/3-5), Potential Loyalists (3-5/2-3/2-3), New Customers (5/1/1), At Risk (1-2/4-5/4-5), Cannot Lose (1-2/4-5/4-5 with high CLV history), Hibernating, Lost — each with a recommended lifecycle treatment.

The data-platform pieces: refresh nightly on a customer-grain table; track segment_membership entered_at + exited_at so move-rate is computable; alert on segment-distribution shifts that exceed historical noise. Used downstream as the segmentation_strategist's primary lens before predictive (lookalike, propensity) models are layered on. Most CDPs (Salesforce Data Cloud, Adobe RT-CDP, Treasure Data, Amperity, ActionIQ) ship RFM as a built-in feature. [REF:cdp.kpi.rfm_distribution_health] [REF:cdp.kpi.segment_move_rate]

Revenue Growth Management (RGM) is the CPG commercial discipline that engineers net revenue across the gross-to-net waterfall — pulling four levers in concert: Price Pack Architecture (PPA), Mix, Promotion Strategy, and Trade Investment. It sits one level above Trade Promotion Management (which is tactical) and one level above Pricing & Promotions (which is retail-facing).

The RGM org typically owns:
• PPA: the structured pack ladder (entry / mainstream / premium / value / super) and the per-unit gap between tiers. [REF:rgm.kpi.mix_index]
• Mix Management: steering channel × pack × tier mix toward higher-margin packs via assortment, distribution, and trade allocation. [REF:rgm.kpi.pack_margin_mix_pct]
• Promo Strategy: mechanic + depth + duration per account × pack × week, scored on Promo Effectiveness Index. [REF:rgm.kpi.promo_effectiveness_index]
• Trade Investment Optimization: annual fund allocation across customers, brands, tiers — accrual vs lump-sum vs pay-for-performance. [REF:rgm.kpi.trade_spend_roi]
• Net Revenue Management: closing the gross-to-net waterfall — list price, off-invoice, rebates, scan-down, bill-back, MCB, slotting, MDF — into a reconciled net revenue under IFRS 15.

Vendor stack: Vistex (settlement) + Pricefx (price optimization) + Model N (channel incentive) + PROS RGM (B2B guidance) + SAP CCM (condition contracts) + Anaplan PM (connected planning) + Periscope by McKinsey / BCG OS RGM toolkit (analytics consulting) + Zilliant / Vendavo (B2B price/margin) + Plytix PIM + Circana / NielsenIQ (mix measurement).

The orgs that run RGM well consistently outperform the category by 200-400 bps of net revenue growth — most of which comes from mix shift, not list price moves.

Price Pack Architecture (PPA) is the structured set of pack sizes, formats, and price points across a brand portfolio engineered to steer consumers up (mix-up) or down (defend against down-trading) within and across channels. The strategic centerpiece of CPG Revenue Growth Management.

The canonical ladder has five tiers:
• entry — small, single-serve or take-home single (test-the-brand pack)
• mainstream — the volume anchor (6-pack / 12-pack)
• premium — higher-margin format (glass bottle, gift box, limited edition)
• value — bigger pack at a sub-pro-rata per-unit (the 18-count value pack)
• super — club channel format (24-, 36-, 48-count)

Three decisions PPA encodes:
1. Pack-tier gap — the per-unit price spacing between adjacent tiers. Too small and you cannibalize; too large and you create down-trade traps.
2. Channel ladder — the per-unit price differential across club / mass / grocery / drug / convenience / ecom. Breaking the ladder (a club exclusive at a lower per-unit) creates consumer arbitrage that's expensive to repair. [REF:rgm.d.channel_ladder]
3. Innovation pack-tier placement — a new SKU launches into a chosen tier; placing it wrong cannibalizes the existing mainstream or fails to extract premium pricing.

The KPI: Mix Index (premium share of volume) [REF:rgm.kpi.mix_index] and Pack Margin Mix % [REF:rgm.kpi.pack_margin_mix_pct]. The data model: pack table separate from product, with ppa_tier and ladder_rank as first-class columns.

Vendor support: Periscope by McKinsey RGM apps + BCG OS RGM toolkit lead in pack-ladder analytics; Pricefx PriceOptimizer and Vistex execute the resulting ladder; SAP CCM persists the condition contracts that derive list prices per tier.

The gross-to-net (GTN) waterfall is the structured ladder of deductions a CPG manufacturer applies between gross sales (units × list price) and reported net revenue. Under IFRS 15, every line in the waterfall is a variable-consideration estimate that must be accrued at sale time and trued-up as it settles.

Canonical lines, top-to-bottom:
1. Gross Revenue — units × list price (the headline number sales loves to talk about)
2. − Off-Invoice Allowance — promo discount applied at ship time
3. − Scan-Down Redemption — credit per unit scanned at POS
4. − Bill-Back — retailer-claimed performance funds
5. − Manufacturer Chargeback (MCB) — credit to wholesaler / distributor passed through to retailer
6. − Rebate Accrual — back-end conditional rebates (volume tiers, JBP)
7. − Slotting Fee Amortization — up-front slotting paid for a new SKU, amortized over 12 months
8. − Marketing Development Funds (MDF) — co-op marketing dollars committed to the account
9. − Returns + Damages provisions
= Net Revenue

Three pain points the warehouse has to solve:
• System-flow reconciliation — list price (SAP CCM), deal terms (Vistex / Pricefx), scan-down (EDI 820 remittance), MCB (Model N), slotting (Finance). Five+ system flows that must reconcile to a single per-account × per-pack × per-period net revenue. [REF:rgm.kpi.price_realization]
• Bi-temporal binding — a deal approved May 15 binds to the list price effective at deal-creation; a shipment May 28 binds to ship-time price. If list changes May 25, the net revenue depends on which price the system bound to.
• Accrual vs settlement gap — the period of 60-180 days between accrual booking and final settlement makes the waterfall a moving target that finance and commercial argue about every close.

The RGM dashboard surfaces this as Price Realization (net / gross) at the SKU × account × period grain. [REF:rgm.kpi.net_revenue_per_unit]

Mix shift is the change in the share of premium vs mainstream vs value packs (or channels, brands, tiers) over a period. It's the dimension of net-revenue variance that's hardest to attribute cleanly because mix interacts with both volume and price.

The canonical variance decomposition is volume × price × mix:
• Volume Variance = (actual_units − plan_units) × plan_net_price [REF:rgm.kpi.volume_variance]
• Price Variance = (actual_net_price − plan_net_price) × actual_units [REF:rgm.kpi.price_variance]
• Mix Variance = Σ((actual_share − plan_share) × (sku_net_price − portfolio_net_price) × total_units) [REF:rgm.kpi.mix_variance]

The math breaks in three ways:
1. SKU crosses PPA tier mid-period — a re-pack moves a SKU from mainstream to premium; naive subtraction parks a residual in 'other' that's unintelligible to the CCO.
2. New pack launches — Innovation Sales % over-credits if cannibalization isn't netted; under-credits if it folds cannibalization in too broadly.
3. Channel ladder shifts — moving volume from grocery to club mechanically improves mix on paper but may destroy margin.

The canonical fix: compute mix variance at the segment_id grain (channel × tier × category); hold plan_share and actual_share as time-stamped policy; rebuild waterfall daily; surface residual as an explicit 'unattributed mix' bucket with drill-through to the offending SKUs.

Vendor stack: Periscope by McKinsey and BCG OS RGM toolkit are the dominant analytics vendors for mix-attribution work; Anaplan PM persists the plan_share policy; Circana Unify / NielsenIQ Connect provide the syndicated mix denominators at 4-8 week latency.

Price Realization is the share of published wholesale list price that actually lands in the manufacturer's books after the gross-to-net waterfall runs.

Formula:
  Price Realization = Σ(net_price × units) / Σ(list_price × units)

A Price Realization of 0.78 means 22% of gross revenue was given back as trade lines (off-invoice + scan-down + bill-back + MCB + rebate accrual + slotting amortization + MDF + returns). The 22% is the 'GTN leakage' the RGM org seeks to compress without losing distribution. [REF:rgm.kpi.price_realization]

Three drivers move it:
1. Trade tactic mix — off-invoice and BOGO compress realization more than conditional rebates and bill-back (pay-for-performance gating).
2. Channel mix — club channel has lower realization than grocery (deeper per-unit discounting to support super packs).
3. List price changes — a list-price increase mechanically improves realization for shipments at unchanged trade terms; the retailer eventually negotiates back via larger off-invoice asks.

The RGM playbook moves Price Realization through:
• Deal-floor enforcement (rgm.d.deal_floor) — reject deals below the per-pack floor at workflow approval.
• Pay-for-performance shift — move trade dollars from off-invoice (1.0 leakage) to conditional rebates (~0.7 effective leakage).
• Pack mix toward premium tiers — premium packs run higher realization because trade dollars are anchored to mainstream tactics.

Vendor surface: Vendavo Profit Analyzer is the industry-standard tool for price-waterfall visualization; Vistex Match Engine and Model N close the settlement loop that feeds the realization calculation back to the dashboard.

Innovation Sales % measures the share of net revenue contributed by SKUs launched in the trailing 24 months. The headline pipeline-effectiveness KPI for the CCO.

Formula:
  Innovation Sales % = Σ(units_skus_launched_24m × net_price) / Σ(total_net_revenue)  [REF:rgm.kpi.innovation_sales_pct]

Industry benchmarks: a 'healthy' innovation pipeline in beverages / snacks / personal care runs 12-22% Innovation Sales %; under 8% the portfolio is stagnating; over 30% there's likely a counting issue.

The counting pitfall — three ways to misreport it:
1. Cannibalization not netted — a value 18-count launches and pulls 60% of its volume from the mainstream 12-count. Reporting Innovation Sales gross of cannibalization over-credits the pipeline.
2. Cannibalization basket too broad — folding in unrelated SKUs under-credits the pipeline. The fix: declare a cannibalization basket per innovation SKU at launch (default: same brand + adjacent pack tiers); compute Innovation Sales % net of basket cannibalization only.
3. 24-month window inclusive vs exclusive — does a SKU launched exactly 730 days ago count? The standard is yes through end-of-month, then off. Persist launch_date and innovation_flag as separate columns on dim_product_rgm and re-derive nightly.

The decision Innovation Sales % feeds: greenlight / kill innovation SKUs based on forecast incremental net revenue, mix-up potential, and cannibalization tolerance. [REF:rgm.d.innovation_priority] The launch business case has to declare the cannibalization basket and tolerance up front, then close the loop in the first 12-week post-launch review.

Available-To-Promise (ATP) is the sellable inventory at a node after subtracting allocated, in-transit-out, and reserved-safety quantities. It is the number the checkout cart can confidently promise the customer in real time, and the central engineering tradeoff in distributed OMS.

Formula at the position grain:
  atp_units = on_hand_units − allocated_units − reserved_safety_units  [REF:oms.kpi.atp_accuracy]

The staleness budget is the sharp edge. Most retailers settle at 30-90 second cache TTL between a store on-hand mutation and the .com inventory cache — fine until a flash promo blows through 30s of demand at one store and the same SKU is over-promised across 100 web sessions. The mitigation pattern is a tiered ATP cache: per-node hot cache plus chain-wide aggregate, with safety reservations sized to the store's peak per-second sell rate, fed by an inventory event bus (Manhattan Active Inventory, Kafka CDC on the WMS) rather than polled snapshots. [REF:oms.d.atp_safety_stock]

ATP feeds the Order Promise Provider (OPP — Manhattan Active Promise, IBM Sterling Promise) which combines sourcing-rule simulation, ATP, carrier transit times, and store pick capacity to quote a delivery promise at checkout. [REF:oms.kpi.promise_vs_actual_gap]

BOPIS Lead Time (Order-to-Ready) is the elapsed minutes from order capture to the line being marked `ready_for_pickup` at the customer's chosen store. Distinct from On-Time Delivery — the SLA clock stops when the customer can collect, not when shipping arrives. [REF:oms.kpi.bopis_lead_time]

Formula:
  BOPIS Lead Time = avg(ready_for_pickup_ts − order_capture_ts)
