Industry benchmarks (Manhattan / Salesforce OMS, apparel + general merch): 2-hour promise for ~70% of orders; 95th percentile under 4 hours; >24-hour tail is store-capacity breach or SKU not-found.

Three levers move it:
1. Sourcing-rule routing — bias BOPIS to stores with available pick capacity, not just the customer's nearest store; per-store throttling at the rule layer is the cleanest control. [REF:oms.d.store_capacity]
2. Store-side pick workflow — handheld pick path optimisation; substitution UX when first-pick fails. First-Pick Fill Rate directly drives BOPIS Lead Time. [REF:oms.kpi.first_pick_fill_rate]
3. Pickup-counter handoff — the in-store pickup scanner has to wire directly to the OMS (POS webhook). 5-15% of BOPIS orders go missing on the pickup-complete event in pre-integration deployments, leaving the line stuck in `ready_for_pickup` for days.

Vendor surface: Manhattan Active Omni, Salesforce OMS, Aptos ONE, and Kibo Order Management all expose store-capacity-aware BOPIS routing; Fluent Commerce's event-sourced model is particularly clean for the pickup-complete reconciliation problem.

Ship-from-Store (SFS) fulfills a .com order out of a physical store's on-hand inventory rather than a DC. Trades higher per-pick labor cost (~$2-4 vs ~$0.40-1.20 at a high-velocity DC) for shorter zone-skip transit time and clearance pull on aging in-store stock.

KPI to watch:
  Ship-from-Store Mix % = store_sourced_lines / total_sourced_lines  [REF:oms.kpi.ship_from_store_mix]

The right target sits in a band — typically 20-35% for apparel / general merch where store-network density compresses last-mile, lower (5-15%) for hardlines / consumables where DC pick economics win. Pushing past the band starts cannibalizing in-store sell-through and trips store-associate labor productivity.

The sourcing decision (oms.d.sourcing_rule) scores candidate nodes against cost-to-serve + speed + capacity + clearance-pull weights — clearance_pull_weight is the dial that intentionally biases sourcing to stores carrying surplus aging inventory. Drain stranded inventory at the store before it becomes a markdown; the OMS rule layer is where merchandising and supply-chain economics actually meet. [REF:oms.kpi.cost_to_serve_per_channel]

Vendor surface: Manhattan Active Omni store fulfillment, IBM Sterling OMS store-fulfillment workflow, Salesforce OMS distributed sourcing, Aptos ONE endless-aisle + SFS, Fluent Commerce rule-based routing — all expose the rule DSL described in oms.d.sourcing_rule.

Distributed Order Management (DOM) is the orchestration layer of an OMS that decides which node fulfills which order line. Inputs are inventory positions across nodes, sourcing rules, capacity, carrier costs, and customer promise; outputs are allocations and ship-from decisions.

The canonical DOM loop:
1. Order captured → candidate nodes enumerated from ATP > 0 at each location for each line.
2. Each candidate scored per the sourcing-rule DSL (Manhattan strategies / Sterling business rules / Fluent GraphQL rules / Salesforce OMS flows) using cost_weight, speed_weight, capacity_weight, and clearance_pull_weight on the winning rule_id.
3. Allocation issued — order_line bound to location_id + rule_id, with estimated_cost_minor + estimated_ready_ts + estimated_delivery_ts captured.
4. Fulfillment events (pick_started → pick_complete → pack → ship → delivered / pickup_complete) flow back through the event log; reallocation triggers if pick fails.

The central economic question DOM answers is the split-shipment tradeoff: a two-line order can be sourced from one DC for a single ship-cost or split across two stores for a faster promise. The cost-versus-promise decision is what makes or breaks per-order margin. The Order Split Rate KPI (orders with multiple shipments / multi-line orders) tracked against a per-banner target band is the right operational signal. [REF:oms.kpi.order_split_rate] [REF:oms.d.split_shipment]

Vendor surface: Manhattan Active Order Management, IBM Sterling OMS, Salesforce OMS, Fluent Commerce, Oracle ROMS, Kibo OMS, Aptos OMS, Deck Commerce — each implements DOM with vendor-specific rule DSLs that lose semantics on cross-vendor migration. A vendor-neutral rule registry plus a translation layer mitigates the drift.

matchSubstring: kpis does the head of omnichannel
response:
The Head of Omnichannel tracks six headline KPIs every week:

1. On-Time Delivery % — orders delivered by promise / orders delivered. The headline customer-trust signal; under 92% the executive review starts. [REF:oms.kpi.on_time_delivery]
2. Promise vs Actual Gap — avg(actual_delivery_ts − promised_delivery_ts) in hours; the per-order leading indicator before OTD drops. [REF:oms.kpi.promise_vs_actual_gap]
3. BOPIS Lead Time — avg(ready_for_pickup_ts − order_capture_ts) in minutes; tracks the store-side execution side of unified commerce. [REF:oms.kpi.bopis_lead_time]
4. Ship-from-Store Mix % — store-sourced lines / total sourced lines; the per-banner economic tradeoff between fulfillment cost and last-mile speed. [REF:oms.kpi.ship_from_store_mix]
5. ATP Accuracy — 1 − mean_abs_error(atp_promised, atp_actual_at_pick); the integrity of inventory promises across the cache tiers. [REF:oms.kpi.atp_accuracy]
6. Cost-to-Serve per Channel — fulfillment_cost / orders by channel; the line that closes the loop between sourcing-rule weights and unit economics. [REF:oms.kpi.cost_to_serve_per_channel]

These feed the central operating decisions: sourcing-rule design (oms.d.sourcing_rule), ATP safety-stock policy (oms.d.atp_safety_stock), split-shipment policy (oms.d.split_shipment), promise-window calibration (oms.d.promise_window), store-capacity throttling (oms.d.store_capacity), and dark-store designation (oms.d.dark_store_designation).

EDI 940 and EDI 945 are the ASC X12 transaction pair that brackets the warehouse leg of the omnichannel order lifecycle.

EDI 940 — Warehouse Shipping Order. Retailer-to-WMS message instructing the warehouse to ship a customer order. Carries the ship-to address, carrier/service-level selection, special handling, and per-line SKU + quantity. The OMS emits one 940 per allocation handed to a 3PL or external WMS; for in-house Manhattan / Blue Yonder WMS the equivalent is typically a Kafka / JMS event rather than a file.

EDI 945 — Warehouse Shipping Advice. WMS-to-retailer confirmation of what actually shipped — serials, weights, dims, tracking number, ship date. This is the message that closes the loop on the OMS allocation status: receipt of a clean 945 moves the order_line from `packed` to `shipped` and feeds Order Cycle Time + On-Time Delivery. [REF:oms.kpi.order_cycle_time] [REF:oms.kpi.on_time_delivery]

Two less-headline but real cousins in the family:
• EDI 943 — Warehouse Stock Transfer Shipment Advice (inter-DC transfers leaving a node).
• EDI 944 — Warehouse Stock Transfer Receipt Advice (inter-DC transfers received at a node).

All four travel over AS2 with PKI; the operational gotcha is partner-id mismatches on the ISA/GS envelope causing silent rejects at the trading-partner gateway. Wire EDI reject codes into the dq_rules layer; a missing 945 inside the carrier-promise window is a fulfillment incident, not a data issue.

Direct Store Delivery (DSD) is the channel where a CPG manufacturer's own (or distributor's) trucks bypass the retailer's DC and drop product straight at the back door of each store — beer, snacks, soft drinks, baked goods, salty snacks, DSD dairy. It collapses three roles into one visit: order writing (presell), physical delivery, and shelf execution (planogram + price tag + rotation).

The operating spine is route accounting: a Master Route is a recurring sequence of stops served on a fixed service-day pattern (MWF). Each day produces a Daily Route — that day's executed instance after Roadnet/Descartes re-optimization. At each stop the driver records cases delivered, returns, spoils, and (in deliver-mode) prints an invoice from the handheld; the customer signs an EPOD and the route settles back at the branch that night against cash + check + EFT + charge-account.

The headline economics are unit-economic: Stops/Route (productivity), Cases/Stop (drop size), Cost/Stop, and Sales/Mile. Below a drop-size threshold a stop flips from deliver-mode to presell+next-day-delivery (or off the route entirely). Above CFR (Case Fill Rate) ~98% retailers stop charging deductions. EDI 894/895 carry the inter-org wire of delivery + signed acknowledgment. [REF:dsd.kpi.cases_per_stop] [REF:dsd.kpi.case_fill_rate]

Presell and deliver are the two operating modes of a DSD route, and the choice is the single most-frequent route-design decision a CPG ops team makes.

Deliver mode (a.k.a. tel-sell-and-deliver, or full-service) — the same driver writes the order at the stop AND delivers product off the truck. One visit, one driver, faster cash. Used where drop size is high enough that the truck-load economics work (large grocery, club, mass DSD beer / soda). Cost-per-stop is high; productivity (stops/day) is low; settlement happens that night.

Presell mode — an Account Sales Rep visits the store one day, writes the order on the handheld, transmits to the branch overnight; a different driver delivers next-day off a bulk truck that's pre-built to a manifest. Two visits, two people, but the delivery driver becomes a courier — 30-50 stops/day instead of 15-20. Used where drop size is too small to justify a full-service deliver call (c-store, drug, small-format grocery).

The flip rule of thumb is drop-size: under ~25 cases/stop, presell+bulk-deliver beats deliver-mode on cost-per-case; over ~40 cases/stop, deliver-mode wins on cycle time and retailer relationship. Hybrid 'merchandiser' routes layer shelf execution on top in either mode. [REF:dsd.kpi.cases_per_stop] [REF:dsd.kpi.cost_per_stop] [REF:dsd.kpi.cost_per_case]

The Director of Route Operations runs DSD off about a dozen metrics, grouped into four families:

Route productivity
1. Stops per Route — count of stops served per route-day; target-banded by route type (deliver 15-25, presell 35-55). [REF:dsd.kpi.stops_per_route]
2. Cases per Stop — total cases delivered ÷ stops; the drop-size lever underneath every route-design decision. [REF:dsd.kpi.cases_per_stop]
3. Drop Size (units) — same denominator, units instead of cases; flips the presell-vs-deliver math. [REF:dsd.kpi.drop_size_units]
4. Driver Productivity (cases/hour) — total cases ÷ paid driver hours. [REF:dsd.kpi.driver_productivity]

Service
5. On-Time Delivery % — stops served inside the retailer's appointment window. [REF:dsd.kpi.on_time_delivery_pct]
6. Case Fill Rate (CFR) — cases delivered ÷ cases ordered; below 98% triggers retailer chargebacks. [REF:dsd.kpi.case_fill_rate]
7. Route Adherence % — stops served in planned sequence. [REF:dsd.kpi.route_adherence_pct]

Shelf execution
8. Perfect Store Score (DSD) — weighted blend of distribution, share-of-cooler, planogram, price compliance, promo, and freshness. [REF:dsd.kpi.perfect_store_score]
9. Out-of-Stock % — share of audited stops with at least one OOS observation. [REF:dsd.kpi.out_of_stock_pct]

Economics & control
10. Cost per Stop — fully-loaded route cost ÷ stops. [REF:dsd.kpi.cost_per_stop]
11. Cost per Case Delivered — fully-loaded route cost ÷ cases. [REF:dsd.kpi.cost_per_case]
12. Sales per Mile — net sales ÷ route miles; the route-design quality metric. [REF:dsd.kpi.sales_per_mile]
13. Settlement Accuracy % — 1 − (settlement variance ÷ total settled). [REF:dsd.kpi.settlement_accuracy]
14. EPOD Capture Rate — share of delivered stops with a signed proof. [REF:dsd.kpi.epod_capture_rate]
15. Returns Rate (DSD) — returned units ÷ delivered units; spikes drag perfect-store. [REF:dsd.kpi.returns_rate]

EDI 894 and EDI 895 are the ASC X12 transaction pair that wires the DSD delivery + signed receipt between a CPG manufacturer and a retailer — the inter-org backbone of every chain DSD account (Walmart, Kroger, Albertsons, Target, 7-Eleven, Sheetz, Circle K).

EDI 894 — Delivery / Return Base Record. Manufacturer-to-retailer message sent at the stop (or end-of-day batched). Carries the GS1 GLN of the delivering location, route id, driver id, GTIN-14 + cases delivered + returned + short, unit and case price, promotional allowance, and the EPOD signature reference. This is the invoice in DSD-land — there is no separate 810 in the deliver-mode flow.

EDI 895 — Delivery / Return Acknowledgement. Retailer-to-manufacturer response posted back from the receiver's WMS/POS system after the store-door receiver scans the load. Confirms what the retailer believes was received line-by-line; any delta becomes a deduction. The 895 is what the manufacturer's deductions team holds up next to the signed EPOD when disputing a back-charge.

The two messages reconcile inside the route-accounting platform (Aldata Apollo, SAP DSD, AFS DSD, Epicor DSD). A common operational gotcha: retailer DCs that net-receive consolidate multiple 895s into a single deduction, breaking the 894↔895↔EPOD trace; the fix is enforcing GS1 SSCC pallet ids on the 894 so the chain reconciles at pallet grain regardless of how the retailer aggregates. [REF:dsd.kpi.epod_capture_rate] [REF:dsd.kpi.settlement_accuracy]

Perfect Store is the CPG / DSD shelf-execution scorecard rolled up to a single per-visit index. In a DSD context (Coca-Cola KO ONE, PepsiCo POWER 5, AB-InBev BEES, Mondelez Perfect Store) it is the metric that gates pay-for-performance trade funds and driver/rep performance bonuses.

The composite is a weighted blend of six sub-scores, each captured by the rep at the audit visit on their Honeywell or Zebra handheld:

1. Distribution — MSL (Must-Stock List) SKUs actually present.
2. Share of Cooler / Share of Shelf — facings of own brand ÷ category facings.
3. Planogram Compliance — facings, blocks, adjacencies vs contracted planogram (Apollo / JDA Space Planning).
4. Price Compliance — observed shelf price tag = contracted price.
5. Promo Compliance — promo POS material up, end-cap built, secondary display priced.
6. Freshness / Code-Date — % of facings inside code-date.

Weights are account-specific (Walmart's contracted weights ≠ Kroger's), held in dim_account.perfect_store_weights, and applied in fct_perfect_store_audits. Below ~85 the rep loses perfect-store credit on that stop; the score also feeds the cooler-replenish and trade-fund decisions. The hardest sub-score to keep clean is planogram — drift creeps in every reset cycle. [REF:dsd.kpi.perfect_store_score]

Driver settlement is the nightly close-out where a DSD driver reconciles the day's load against the day's deliveries + returns + spoils + collected cash/check/EFT/charge — and the cage signs off (or sends it back). It is the single most error-prone process in DSD and the one most regulators scrutinise.

The ledger is built in the route-accounting platform (Aldata Apollo, SAP DSD, AFS DSD, Epicor DSD):

  Total Invoiced  =  Total Cash + Check + EFT + Charge Account
                   + Returns Credit + Spoilage Credit + Variance

Variance is the open balance the driver owes (or is owed). Settlement Accuracy % = 1 − (variance ÷ invoiced) and the target is > 99%. Sub-99 settlement nights typically trace to two patterns: (1) miskeyed returns at the stop — the driver entered cases when the retailer scanned units; (2) collected funds that never made it back to the bag — the cash-handling control point.

The controls are SOX-bracketed in any public CPG: dual-count at the cage, blind audit on a sample of routes, exception report on any variance > $20, and EPOD-backed dispute for any retailer-side deduction inside the invoice. [REF:dsd.kpi.settlement_accuracy]

matchSubstring: five steps of the s&op cycle
response:
Oliver Wight's classic five-step S&OP cycle (now Integrated Business Planning) repeats every calendar month:

1. Product Review — gate decisions on new product introductions, end-of-life, line extensions. The product portfolio is locked for the cycle.
2. Demand Review — reconcile the statistical baseline forecast with sales-driven, marketing-driven, and finance-aligned views into one consensus number. [REF:sop.kpi.forecast_accuracy_mape]
3. Supply Review — net the consensus demand against capacity, inventory, and lead-times; surface supply-demand gaps and any constraint violations. [REF:sop.kpi.capacity_utilization]
4. Integrated Reconciliation — finance + commercial + supply close out the cross-functional gaps; a working capital and gap-to-budget statement is produced. [REF:sop.kpi.working_capital_impact]
5. Management Business Review (MBR / Executive S&OP) — COO / CSCO signs off the consensus plan, interventions, and capex asks. [REF:sop.kpi.plan_adherence]

In SAP IBP, Kinaxis RapidResponse, o9, Blue Yonder, Oracle SCP, and Anaplan all five steps share one item-location-period data model so each gate inherits the signed-off state of the prior gate.

MAPE and WAPE both measure forecast accuracy as percentage error, but they differ in how they aggregate:

MAPE — Mean Absolute Percentage Error = mean of abs(actual − forecast) / actual across rows. Treats every row equally regardless of volume. Breaks down when actual is near zero (division blows up). Use for like-for-like comparisons across well-trafficked SKUs.

WAPE — Weighted Absolute Percentage Error = sum(abs(actual − forecast)) / sum(actual). Volume-weighted; large-volume items dominate the number. Robust to near-zero actuals. The preferred headline accuracy KPI in retail and CPG because it correctly down-weights the long tail.

In practice S&OP / IBP teams track both: WAPE as the corporate headline because it ties to dollars, MAPE per-SKU to surface forecast-quality outliers. [REF:sop.kpi.forecast_accuracy_mape] [REF:sop.kpi.forecast_accuracy_wape]

Kinaxis RapidResponse (Maestro) and SAP IBP are the two most-deployed enterprise S&OP / IBP platforms. They differ in worldview:

Kinaxis RapidResponse — concurrent-planning architecture. Every plan change recalculates the network in seconds; scenarios are first-class and can be branched, merged, compared. Strong for high-mix discrete manufacturers (automotive, aerospace, semiconductor) where the value is in seeing the ripple. Scenario Cycle Time is the headline KPI. [REF:sop.kpi.scenario_cycle_time]

SAP IBP — natively integrated with SAP S/4HANA ERP master data, time-series + order-based planning, deep MEIO and demand sensing modules. Strong for process manufacturers running on SAP backbone (CPG, life sciences, chemicals). Stronger out-of-the-box for finance-aligned IBP since the financial reconciliation already maps to S/4HANA controlling.

Both speak the same SCOR / Oliver Wight five-step vocabulary. The choice is usually: which ERP backbone are you on (Kinaxis is ERP-agnostic; IBP is SAP-aligned), and how scenario-heavy is your planning process.

Multi-Echelon Inventory Optimization (MEIO) jointly sets safety stock across plant → DC → forward-DC → store tiers, recognising that pooling inventory at higher tiers reduces total network inventory for the same service level.

The core insight (Graves & Willems, 2008): if you set safety stock independently per tier, you over-stock at every tier because variances compound. MEIO solves the joint problem — for a target OTIF, what's the minimum-cost distribution of safety stock across all tiers?

Vendors: ToolsGroup SO99+, GAINSystems, Logility InventoryAI+, Kinaxis RapidResponse Inventory, SAP IBP Inventory, o9 inventory app. They differ on the optimization technique (analytic vs. simulation vs. probabilistic) and the demand model (deterministic with safety stock vs. stochastic with service-level constraint).

The headline outcome: 15-30% inventory reduction for the same service level in a typical multi-tier CPG / retail network. [REF:sop.kpi.inventory_turns] [REF:sop.kpi.working_capital_impact]

matchSubstring: how do we measure forecast bias
response:
Forecast Bias measures systematic over- or under-forecast. Computed as sum(forecast − actual) / sum(actual), expressed as a signed percentage:

• Bias = 0% — unbiased forecast.
• Bias > 0% — over-forecast, leading to excess inventory and write-downs.
• Bias < 0% — under-forecast, leading to stockouts and lost service.

The target band is typically ±2% over a rolling 13-week window. Persistent bias is a planner-behaviour signal: sales pushes forecast up to ensure supply ('insurance forecasting'), so consensus runs hot; or marketing pushes up to fund promotion plans that don't materialise. The IBP Integrated Reconciliation step exists to detect and de-bias these contributions before they hit the supply plan. [REF:sop.kpi.forecast_bias]

A companion metric — bias decomposed by item ABC class and by forecast_version — surfaces which contributors are biased and where. The standard report is bias-by-version stacked against MAPE.

matchSubstring: kpis does the vp of reverse logistics care
response:
The VP of Reverse Logistics tracks seven core KPIs weekly:

1. Return Rate % — returned units ÷ sold units. The headline of the whole function; ecom apparel runs 25-40%, electronics 8-15%. [REF:rrl.kpi.return_rate_pct]
2. Refund Issue Time — days from RMA receipt to refund issued. Correlates with CSAT and chargeback rate. [REF:rrl.kpi.refund_issue_time]
3. Recovery Value % — recovery proceeds ÷ original COGS of returned inventory. The net-economic outcome of the disposition tree. [REF:rrl.kpi.recovery_value_pct]
4. Refurb Yield % — units coming back as A/B-grade ÷ units sent to refurb. Below threshold, refurb routing is uneconomic vs straight liquidation. [REF:rrl.kpi.refurb_yield_pct]
5. Cost to Process Return — fully loaded reverse-leg cost per item. The threshold below which a returnless-refund decision flips positive. [REF:rrl.kpi.cost_to_process_return]
6. Return Fraud Rate — fraud-flagged returns ÷ total returns. Drives policy tightening and Appriss/Newmine step-up gating. [REF:rrl.kpi.return_fraud_rate]
7. Net Cost of Returns — refunds + processing cost − recovery proceeds. The board-level P&L line. [REF:rrl.kpi.net_cost_of_returns]

These feed three decisions: disposition route (restock / refurb / liquidate / donate / recycle / scrap / returnless), returnless threshold, and CRC routing.

A returnless refund (Amazon's 'keep it' refund) is a disposition where the customer is refunded but not asked to send the item back. The decision is purely economic — when forward shipping + inspection + restock + refurb cost exceeds the unit's recovery value, it's cheaper to issue the refund and let the customer keep the item.

The threshold typically lands between $15-$40 unit cost, depending on category. The KPI is Returnless Refund Rate — returnless refunds ÷ total refunds — tuned in a 5-15% band against return fraud rate. [REF:rrl.kpi.returnless_refund_rate]

Mechanically, the returnless decision lives in the returns engine (Optoro OptiTurn, SAP S/4 Advanced Returns, Loop) and references unit COGS + carrier rate card + historical disposition yield. The associated refund row carries refund_type = 'returnless'; the return_item never materializes because no physical reverse leg happens.

Fraud exposure is real — a small share of returnless-eligible customers learn the trigger and game it. Newmine and Appriss Retail score repeat returners against returnless eligibility before the engine releases the refund.

matchSubstring: return rate down to the source-system field
response:
Return Rate % lineage in five hops:

Step 1 — KPI definition: Return Rate % = return_items_units / sold_units, computed at the BI layer. [REF:rrl.kpi.return_rate_pct]
Step 2 — Mart fact: fct_returns_rrl carries return_item_id at the disposition grain; fct_returns_rrl + dim_date_rrl + dim_product_rrl deliver the numerator. fct_sales (from merchandising or omnichannel_oms) delivers sold_units.
Step 3 — dbt intermediate: int_returns_reverse_logistics__hub_return_item + int_returns_reverse_logistics__link_item_rma reconcile each returned unit to its parent RMA.
Step 4 — Staging: stg_returns_reverse_logistics__return_items materializes the source.returns_reverse_logistics.return_item table from the populated DuckDB.
Step 5 — Source system: return_item rows originate in the returns portal (Loop / Narvar / Happy Returns webhook), in the OMS Returns module (Salesforce OMS Returns, Manhattan Active WMS Returns), or in the ERP returns engine (SAP S/4 Advanced Returns Management) via EDI 180 RMA.

Field-level: EDI 180 RMA → connector conn.edi_x12_180 → Kafka topic returns.rma.v1 → raw layer → stg_returns_reverse_logistics__return_authorizations.

BORIS — Buy Online, Return In Store — is the omnichannel return method where a customer returns a .com order at a physical store of the same chain. Operationally it compresses the reverse leg (no carrier transit) and lifts attach-sale rate; surveyed BORIS visits convert to an incremental purchase ~25% of the time.

Date-flow: the customer presents the order at the in-store returns counter (or self-service kiosk); the POS / OMS Returns module performs an order_lookup against the omnichannel golden record; the RMA is created with return_method = 'boris_store' and rma_status = 'received' in a single transaction; the refund is typically issued to original tender on the spot. The physical unit lands in the store's returns staging until the next backhaul to the CRC or an in-store restock decision.

KPI impact: BORIS lifts Return Rate % visibility (more returns are captured to the chain rather than abandoned), compresses Refund Issue Time, and lifts Recovery Value % because store-pickable A-Grade units restock the local shelf without a CRC round-trip. [REF:rrl.kpi.refund_issue_time] [REF:rrl.kpi.recovery_value_pct]

Reference platforms: Manhattan Active WMS Returns, Salesforce OMS Returns, SAP S/4 Advanced Returns.

The disposition decision tree determines what happens to each return_item once it lands at the CRC (Centralized Return Center). Most large retailers run a six-or-seven-way tree:

1. **restock_A** — A-Grade unit, sellable as new. Goes back to the forward DC or a Optoro forward-stocked returns pool. Best recovery.
2. **restock_open_box** — minor cosmetic but functional. Routed to an open-box channel (Best Buy Open Box, Amazon Warehouse).
3. **refurb** — needs cleaning / testing / repackaging / firmware-flash. Routed to a CRC refurb cell or 3PL (Optoro, Newmine). [REF:rrl.kpi.refurb_yield_pct]
4. **b_stock_liquidation** — unsellable-as-new, sold in bulk lots through B-Stock / Liquidation.com / BULQ. [REF:rrl.kpi.liquidation_yield_pct]
5. **donation** — functional but unsellable, donated to Good360 / Delivering Good for tax-deductible value.
6. **recycle** — sent to a certified recycler (driven by EPR regulation in EU and California).
7. **scrap** — destroyed. The economic floor.

The tree is scored on Recovery Value % minus reverse-leg + processing cost. Returnless refund (refund issued without the item coming back) is the upstream branch that bypasses the tree entirely.

matchSubstring: kpis does the category manager care
response:
The Category Manager tracks seven core KPIs every week, all read off the syndicated panel + EDI 852 feeds:

1. Category Sales Growth % — period-over-period growth at the banner; headline outcome for the category business plan. [REF:cm.kpi.category_sales_growth_pct]
2. Distribution Coverage (%ACV) — ACV-weighted share of stores carrying the SKU. 80%+ is national broad. [REF:cm.kpi.distribution_coverage_pct]
3. Range Productivity — sales $ ÷ # listed SKUs. The breadth-vs-efficiency tradeoff at every range review. [REF:cm.kpi.range_productivity]
4. Shelf Space Productivity — sales $ ÷ linear ft allocated. Drives reallocation across categories at fixture re-cuts. [REF:cm.kpi.shelf_space_productivity]
5. Assortment Compliance % — stores carrying mandated SKUs ÷ stores audited; below 90% silently kills range-review uplift. [REF:cm.kpi.assortment_compliance_pct]
6. Velocity Index — SKU units/store/week ÷ category average; >1.0 earns above-fair-share facings. [REF:cm.kpi.velocity_index]
7. Innovation Slot % — SKUs launched <24 months ÷ total SKUs; target band differs sharply by category. [REF:cm.kpi.innovation_slot_pct]

These feed three flagship decisions: assortment optimization, planogram authoring, and the range review.

Role of Category is the ECR-blueprint classification a retailer applies to every category — Destination, Routine, Occasional/Seasonal, or Convenience — that drives shelf-space allocation, pricing posture, promo intensity, and assortment depth.

• **Destination** — categories the retailer wants shoppers to choose the banner *specifically* for. Deepest assortment, sharpest price, heaviest promo, most space. Typical banner has 5-10. (For Costco: bulk pantry; for Whole Foods: prepared foods.)
• **Routine** — categories needed to make the trip basket complete. Competitive-parity assortment, normal margin, normal promo. The largest share of categories by count.
• **Occasional/Seasonal** — visited intermittently (BBQ, back-to-school, holiday). Disproportionate basket and margin at peak; minimal space at trough. Range cycles seasonally.
• **Convenience** — fill-in categories not driving trip choice. Narrow assortment, minimum space, no deep promo.

The role assignment is the foundational input to the category business plan; it gets reviewed annually at the joint manufacturer/retailer JBP. The wrong role assignment silently misallocates ~20% of category investment for the year. [REF:cm.kpi.category_share_of_wallet]

Blue Yonder Category Management (formerly JDA Space Planning, originally Intactix) is the dominant planogram-authoring platform in the world — >50% global market share among the top-100 retailers. The suite covers four modules:

1. **Space Planning** — the planogram editor. SKU placement, facings, shelf/slot grain, block adjacencies, vertical/horizontal flow. Takes pack dimensions from the GS1 GDSN attribute pool and applies fair-share or productivity-weighted facing algorithms.
2. **Floor Planning** — store-level fixture layout; categories arranged on the store floor with adjacency rules and traffic-flow constraints.
3. **Assortment Optimization** — recommends SKU adds/drops/mandates under shelf-space and must-stock constraints, scored on net category sales impact.
4. **Open Space Suite** — the Open Space Initiative version exposes APIs for embedded planogram serving.

Competitors: Symphony RetailAI Category Manager (CINDE AI), RELEX Solutions Space Planning, Quad Tag Galleria (visual planogram), SAP CAR/RPC. Industry trends: AI-driven autonomous planogram updates (Symphony CINDE, Blue Yonder's Cognitive POG), image-recognition shelf compliance (Trax, Salesforce CG Cloud, AFS) closing the loop between planogram-as-authored and planogram-as-executed. [REF:cm.kpi.shelf_space_productivity]

The CPG / Retail category-management discipline runs on five canonical syndicated data sources:

1. **Circana** (Information Resources Inc + NPD Group merged 2023) — Unify / Liquid Data / Total Category View. POS scan-track + Consumer Network panel (receipt-based). The Coke vs Pepsi default for U.S. food.
2. **NielsenIQ Connect** + **Discover** + **Spectra** + **Homescan** — POS scan-track measurement, pricing/promo intelligence, consumer demand, household panel. The Unilever / P&G default for international + select U.S.
3. **Numerator** — receipt-based purchase panel (InfoScout + Market Track lineage). Strongest for cross-banner basket affinity and walk-away analysis.
4. **Kantar Worldpanel** — global FMCG household panel. Penetration, frequency, switching, basket affinity at household grain.
5. **dunnhumby** — Customer Data Science (Tesco / Kroger 84.51° lineage). Loyalty-data-driven range and assortment science.

Plus **GfK** (part of NielsenIQ since 2023) for durables and tech panel. These are licensed feeds — the manufacturer does not own the raw retailer data. Coverage varies sharply by retailer (Walmart and Amazon are notoriously sparse in syndicated coverage; Kroger covered via 84.51° rather than syndicated). The denominator definition (Total US Food vs Total US Multi-Outlet+) materially changes any share number — always confirm before quoting. [REF:cm.kpi.category_share_of_wallet]

A range review is the recurring (seasonal or annual) cycle in which a retailer and its category captain (or the broader manufacturer set) jointly review velocity, share, margin, and cannibalization to decide SKU adds, drops, mandates, repacks, and reclasses for the category. The output drives the next planogram cycle.

The canonical workflow:

1. **Pre-read** — pull 52-week velocity, distribution, share, and margin for every listed SKU from syndicated POS (Circana / NielsenIQ) + retailer EDI 852.
2. **Gold/Silver/Bronze tiering** — rank SKUs by composite productivity; bottom quintile (Bronze) becomes kill-candidate. [REF:cm.kpi.velocity_index]
3. **Must-have walk-away check** — for every kill candidate, run household-panel walk-away (Kantar / Numerator) — would the shopper leave the banner if this SKU disappeared?
4. **Innovation slot allocation** — how many of the Bronze kills get refilled by new launches vs reclaim of facings. [REF:cm.kpi.innovation_slot_pct]
5. **Cannibalization check** — for every new listing, estimate share-source from existing range; net-incremental = new_units × (1 − cannibalization_rate). [REF:cm.kpi.cannibalization_rate]
6. **Retailer presentation** — the captain presents the recommended assortment + planogram + forecast P&L. The buyer approves or amends.
7. **In-market execution** — planogram resets cascade to stores; assortment compliance audits begin 4-6 weeks later. [REF:cm.kpi.assortment_compliance_pct]

Decision time horizon: 3-6 months from pre-read to in-market. Reference platforms: Symphony Category Manager + CINDE, Blue Yonder Assortment Optimization, RELEX Assortment, dunnhumby Range Science.

matchSubstring: how do i calculate scope 3 emissions per spend dollar
response:
Scope 3 Category 1 (Purchased Goods & Services) and Category 2 (Capital Goods) emissions per dollar of spend is the headline procurement-sustainability KPI disclosed under CSRD ESRS E1 and CDP. The calculation is a hybrid hierarchy at the PO-line grain.

Formula:
  Scope 3 Intensity = Σ(po_line.scope3_kgco2e) / Σ(po_line.line_amount_base_usd)  [REF:psa.kpi.scope3_intensity]

Apply the best-available factor per line in this strict order — never sum across tiers for the same line:

1. **Primary supplier data** — supplier discloses CO2e per unit shipped via CDP Supply Chain, EcoVadis Carbon Action, or direct Product Carbon Footprint (PCF). Apply line-by-line where supplier_id has an active disclosure for the reporting year. Covers <15% of suppliers by count but typically >50% of spend in mature programs.
2. **Activity-based factor** — emissions_factor_kgco2e_per_unit × po_line.quantity. Uses ecoinvent / EXIOBASE / USEEIO activity factors keyed on UNSPSC commodity + country_iso2 + vintage_year. Requires UoM normalization (kg, liter, kWh, piece) — uncertainty_pct typically ±25-40%.
3. **Spend-based factor** — emissions_factor_kgco2e_per_usd × line_amount_base_usd. EXIOBASE/USEEIO spend-based factors per UNSPSC commodity × country. Coarsest tier (uncertainty ±50-80%) but the only option for free-text lines and tail suppliers.

Four gotchas to ship-proof the calc:
• **Factor vintage** — EXIOBASE/USEEIO factors typically lag 2-3 years; track emissions_factor.vintage_year and disclose the gap to current-period spend in CSRD reporting.
• **Corporate-family roll-up** — supplier discloses at parent_duns, transactions hit local DUNS subsidiaries; allocate parent emissions pro-rata to child spend.
• **Capital goods boundary** — Category 2 covers depreciable capital, not consumables; gate by category_taxonomy.capex_or_opex.
• **Double-count check** — never sum primary + activity + spend for the same line; the hierarchy is strict pick-one.

Vendor surface: Sievo Emissions Analytics, EcoVadis Carbon Action Module, Watershed for Procurement, Persefoni Carbon Hub, and IBM Envizi Supply Chain Intelligence all implement this hierarchy. CDP Supply Chain is the dominant primary-data feed (~30K suppliers globally as of 2026).

The Chief Procurement Officer tracks seven headline KPIs every month:

1. Spend Under Management — sum(spend_with_active_contract_or_strategic_sourced) / sum(total_spend). Best-in-class >85% direct, >70% indirect; the maturity measure for the procurement function. [REF:psa.kpi.spend_under_mgmt]
2. Savings Realized % — sum(savings_realized) / sum(savings_committed). Below 70% means contracted savings aren't sticking and contract-leakage controls need work. [REF:psa.kpi.savings_realized_pct]
3. Maverick Spend % — sum(off_contract_spend) / sum(total_addressable_spend). Top decile sits at 2-5%; >15% means coverage controls are broken. [REF:psa.kpi.maverick_spend_pct]
4. Supplier Risk Score (weighted) — composite of financial / geographic / cyber / compliance / sustainability per supplier; lower = riskier. [REF:psa.kpi.supplier_risk_score]
5. Scope 3 Intensity (Cat 1+2) — kgCO2e per USD spent; the board-disclosed line under CSRD ESRS E1. [REF:psa.kpi.scope3_intensity]
6. Touchless PO % — POs with zero human-approval steps. >70% on indirect tail is world-class. [REF:psa.kpi.touchless_po_pct]
7. Sustainable Spend % — spend with EcoVadis Silver+ suppliers / total addressable spend. ESG commitment KPI; target 75-90% by 2030 against SBTi. [REF:psa.kpi.sustainable_spend_pct]

These feed eight headline decisions: supplier consolidation (psa.d.consolidate_suppliers), renegotiate-now (psa.d.negotiate_now), terminate-supplier (psa.d.terminate_supplier), payment-terms (psa.d.renegotiate_payment_terms), tail-spend strategy (psa.d.tail_spend_strategy), Scope 3 engagement (psa.d.scope3_supplier_engagement), maverick response (psa.d.maverick_spend_response), and contract renewal (psa.d.contract_renewal).

Maverick spend is procurement transacted outside negotiated contracts for a category the procurement org has covered — buyer goes around the contract, pays list price, and forfeits the negotiated terms (rebates, payment terms, sustainability clauses).

Formula:
  Maverick Spend % = sum(off_contract_spend) / sum(total_addressable_spend)  [REF:psa.kpi.maverick_spend_pct]

Detection at ingest — the maverick_flag on po_line is materialized by joining (supplier_id, category_code, transaction_date) to active contract scope. If no covering contract exists for the supplier-category-date triple, flag the line.

Three common patterns:
1. P-card breach — buyer uses corporate purchasing card for an item that has a catalog contract. Mitigated by tightening P-card merchant-category-code (MCC) restrictions.
2. Free-text PO — buyer enters a free-text PO line for an item that punches out to a contracted catalog. ML categorization at ingest catches the most common cases.
3. Marketplace bypass — buyer transacts via Amazon Business for office supplies that have an active Staples contract. Either consolidate the marketplace under contracted-supplier coverage or close the bypass.

Response playbook (psa.d.maverick_spend_response): tier 1 — block the PO submission outright (zero-tolerance covered categories); tier 2 — route to category-manager approval with justification field; tier 3 — absorb the transaction but convert to a future PO with the contracted supplier; tier 4 — track and dashboard, no block.

Vendor surface: SAP Ariba Buying Policy + Catalog Compliance, Coupa Buying Channel Rules, Ivalua Catalog Management — all expose the rule DSL needed for the four-tier response.

Three-way match is the AP control that auto-clears a supplier invoice only when three documents agree on quantity, unit price, and amount within configured tolerance bands:

1. Purchase Order — the contract-of-the-transaction; what was agreed to buy at what price.
2. Goods Receipt — confirmation from the receiving plant that the goods physically arrived at the agreed quantity.
3. Supplier Invoice — the AP-side document the supplier sends asking for payment.

When all three reconcile within tolerance (typically $50 / 2% per line), the invoice auto-flows to payment. That's the headline AP automation case — captured in the invoice_match_rate KPI. [REF:psa.kpi.invoice_match_rate]

In practice, 10-25% of invoices fail the match — the AP exception queue. Three primary failure legs:
• PO-Invoice price variance — supplier billed a different price than the PO; usually a contract-price refresh that hasn't propagated.
• PO-Receipt quantity variance — over-receipt or under-receipt vs PO; partial shipments break the match unless tolerance allows split.
• Missing receipt — invoice arrived before the goods receipt was posted; common on services where receipts are weak.

Two-way match (PO + invoice, no receipt) is the relaxation used for services, subscription invoices, and low-value catalog spend where goods-receipt would cost more than the control gains. The tolerance threshold decision (psa.d.invoice_match_threshold) is where finance and AP set the dial — typically auto-pay <$2,500 with 2-way; require 3-way above that.

Vendor surface: SAP S/4HANA AP Invoice Match, Oracle Cloud ERP Payables, Basware AP Automation, Tradeshift Pay, and Coupa Invoice all implement configurable tolerance bands per category + supplier. Exception-queue routing with provenance (which leg failed) is the operational lift on AP cost.

Tail spend is the bottom-80% of suppliers by spend — typically ~5% of total spend but the source of most supplier-master workload, most maverick spend, and the worst sustainable-procurement coverage. The CPO target is to compress it without exploding sourcing labor.

Metric:
  Tail Spend % = sum(spend_in_bottom_80pct_suppliers) / sum(total_spend)  [REF:psa.kpi.tail_spend_pct]

Four playbook moves (psa.d.tail_spend_strategy):

1. Catalog + Punchout Consolidation — corral 60-70% of indirect tail spend onto a handful of catalog/punchout suppliers (Staples, Amazon Business, Grainger, Office Depot, Fisher Scientific). The buying_channel_mix KPI tracks the shift. [REF:psa.kpi.buying_channel_mix]
2. P-Card Programs — push low-value (<$2,500) high-frequency transactions onto purchasing cards with category-restricted MCCs. Trades AP workload for card fees (1.5-2%) but compresses cycle time to seconds.
3. Marketplace (Amazon Business, Mercateo Unite) — route the genuine long tail to a marketplace channel with pre-negotiated terms across thousands of small suppliers; the marketplace becomes supplier-of-record for spend cube purposes.
4. Auto-PO with Rules — for predictable repeat tail (e.g. recurring services, monthly subscriptions), set rule-based auto-PO with the contracted supplier; zero requisition required.

The headline KPI movement: well-run tail-spend programs compress tail from 8% to 3% of spend over 18-24 months while concentrating supplier count from 5,000 to 1,500.

Vendor surface: SAP Ariba Spot Buy, Coupa Marketplace, Tradeshift Buy, Amazon Business Punchout, and Mercateo Unite — all expose pre-negotiated catalog-style buying without per-supplier contract overhead.

Supplier Risk Score is a 0..100 weighted composite combining five dimensions per supplier — lower scores meaning higher risk. It's the headline criticality KPI tracked by the Supplier Risk Manager and the trigger for termination, dual-sourcing, and watchlist decisions.

Formula:
  supplier_risk_score = weighted(financial, geographic, cyber, compliance, sustainability)
  [REF:psa.kpi.supplier_risk_score]

The five dimensions, each normalised to 0..100:
1. Financial — D&B Paydex (payment behavior) + Failure Score percentile; a dropping Paydex is the leading indicator of distress, often 6-18 months ahead of bankruptcy.
2. Geographic — Resilinc EventWatchAI + Sphera SCRM real-time event exposure (fire, flood, port closure, geopolitical incident); weighted higher for single-source critical suppliers.
3. Cyber — BitSight or RiskRecon composite (250..900 scale, normalized); board-level concern after major OT/IT supplier breaches.
4. Compliance — OFAC / UN / EU sanctions exposure plus internal questionnaire results (Modern Slavery Act, anti-bribery, conflict minerals).
5. Sustainability — EcoVadis composite + CDP climate score; flags Net-Zero risk + CSRD reporting exposure.

The central engineering challenge is correlation across sources: each vendor uses a different supplier identifier, score scale, and refresh cadence. Mitigation — normalize every source to 0..100 first, align on D&B DUNS as canonical join key with name+country fuzzy fallback, then alert on score delta rather than absolute level. A 15-point Paydex drop matters more than the absolute number.

The response decision (psa.d.terminate_supplier) — terminate if composite < 30 and a dual-source is available; watchlist + corrective-action-plan if 30-50; monitor if 50-70; no action if >70. Category criticality weight modifies the threshold — single-source critical suppliers get a higher floor.

SAP Ariba and Coupa are the two dominant Source-to-Pay platforms, with Jaggaer, Ivalua, GEP, and Zycus rounding out the named-vendor field. They differ in product-architecture worldview:

SAP Ariba — the incumbent. Ariba Network (5M+ suppliers globally), Sourcing, Contracts, SLP (Supplier Lifecycle & Performance), and Buying & Invoicing. Deepest integration with SAP S/4HANA ERP backbones — natively reads MM, FI, and CO master data. The default at enterprises running SAP-stack ERP. Weak spots — UI ergonomics, slower release cadence, and per-supplier transaction fees on the Network.

Coupa Business Spend Management Platform — the challenger, born cloud-first. Procure / Sourcing / Contracts / Risk Assess / Travel & Expense in one platform; Coupa Pay handles supplier payments. Strong AI-driven anomaly detection (Coupa Power Apps). The default at enterprises seeking ERP-agnostic S2P with strong T&E integration. Weak spots — narrower supplier network than Ariba, weaker direct-materials depth.

The choice usually comes down to: ERP backbone (Ariba is SAP-aligned; Coupa is ERP-agnostic), supplier-network density (Ariba leads), AP automation depth (Coupa Pay vs Ariba Buying & Invoicing — neck and neck), and total-spend-managed scope (Coupa wins on T&E integration).

Deeper specialist alternatives — Jaggaer ONE (academia + government strength), Ivalua (direct materials + manufacturing strength), GEP SMART (consulting-led implementations), and Zycus (AI-led negotiation via Merlin) — all surface the same UNSPSC + DUNS-anchored data model the spend cube needs.
